Question: A temperature difference can generate e.m.f. in some materials. Let S be the e.m.f. produced per unit temperature difference between the ends of a wire, $\sigma $ the electrical conductivity and $k$ the thermal conductivity of the material of the wire. Taking$M,L,T,I$ and $K$ as dimensions of mass, length, time, current and temperature, respectively, the dimensional formula of the quantity $Z = \frac{{{S^2}\sigma }}{k}$ is:
Solution:
S = emf per unit temperature difference
$\sigma = $Electrical conductivity
k = Thermal conductivity
$\left[ S \right] = \left[ {M{L^2}{T^{ - 3}}{I^{ - 1}}{K^{ - 1}}} \right]$
$\left[ \sigma \right] = \left[ {{M^{ - 1}}{L^{ - 3}}{T^3}{I^2}} \right]$
$\left[ K \right] = \left[ {{M^1}{L^1}{T^{ - 3}}{K^{ - 1}}} \right]$
$\left[ Z \right] = \frac{{{S^2}\sigma }}{K} = \frac{{\left[ {{M^1}{L^1}{T^{ - 3}}{K^{ - 2}}} \right]}}{{\left[ {{M^1}{L^1}{T^{ - 3}}{K^{ - 1}}} \right]}}$
$\left[ Z \right] = \left[ {{K^{ - 1}}} \right]$

Question: A projectile of mass 200 g is launched in a viscous medium at an angle $60^\circ $ with the horizontal, with an initial velocity of 270 m/s. It experiences a viscous drag force $\vec F =  - c\vec v$ where the drag coefficient $c = 0.1kg/s$ and $\vec v$ is the instantaneous velocity of the projectile. The projectile hits a vertical wall after 2s. Taking $e = 2.7,$ the horizontal distance of the wall from the point of projection (in m) is ____
Solution:
${\vec F_{net}} = m\frac{{d\vec v}}{{dt}}$
$m\vec g + \vec F = \frac{{md\vec v}}{{dt}}$
$m\vec g - C\vec v = \frac{{md\vec v}}{{dt}}$
Horizontal direction
$ - C{v_x} = \frac{{md{v_x}}}{{dt}}$
$ - \frac{C}{m}\int\limits_0^t {dt} = \int\limits_{{v_{0x}}}^{{v_x}} {\frac{{d{v_x}}}{{{v_x}}}} $
$ - \frac{t}{2} = \ell n\frac{{{v_x}}}{{{v_{0x}}}}$
$\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = {v_x} = {v_{0x}}{e^{ - t/2}}$
$\int\limits_0^{{S_x}} {dx} = {v_{0x}}\int\limits_0^t {{e^{ - t/2}}dt} $
${S_x} = 2{v_{0x}}\left( {1 - {e^{ - t/2}}} \right)$
At $t = 2\sec $
${S_x} = 2 \times 270 \times \cos 60^\circ \left[ {1 - \frac{1}{e}} \right]$
${S_x} = 270\left( {1 - \frac{1}{{2.7}}} \right)$
$ = \frac{{270}}{{2.7}}\left( {1.7} \right) = 170m$
${S_x} = 170m$

Question: Consider a star of mass ${m_2}$ kg revolving in a circular orbit around another start of mass ${m_1}kg$ with ${m_1} >  > {m_2}.$ The heavier start slowly acquires mass from the lighter star at a constant rate of $\gamma $ kg/s. In this transfer process, there is no other loss of mass. If the separation between the centers of the stars is r, then its relative rate of change $\frac{1}{r}\frac{{dr}}{{dt}}\left( {in\,{s^{ - 1}}} \right)$ is given by:
Solution:
${m_2}{\omega ^2}r = \frac{{G{m_1}{m_2}}}{{{r^2}}}$
$\omega = \sqrt {\frac{{G{m_1}}}{{{r^3}}}} $
$L = {m_2}\omega {r^2}$
$ = {m_2}\sqrt {\frac{{G{m_1}}}{{{r^3}}}} {r^2}$
\[L = {m_2}\sqrt {G{m_1}r} \]= const.
$\ell nL = \ell n{m_2} + \ell nG + \frac{1}{2}\ell n{m_1} + \frac{1}{2}\ell nr$
$0 = \frac{{d{m_2}}}{{{m_2}}} + \frac{1}{2}\frac{{d{m_1}}}{{{m_1}}} + \frac{1}{2}\frac{{dr}}{r}$
$\frac{{dr}}{{rdt}} = - \frac{{2d{m_2}}}{{{m_2}dt}} - \frac{{d{m_1}}}{{{m_1}dt}} \approx - \frac{{2\gamma }}{{{m_2}}}$ Here $\left( {\frac{{dm}}{{dt}} = \gamma } \right)$

Question: As shown in the figures, a uniform rod $OO'$ of length $l$ is hinged at the point O and held in place vertically between two walls using two massless springs of same spring constant. The springs are connected at the midpoint and at the top-end $\left( {O'} \right)$ of the rod, as shown in Fig. 1 and the rod is made to oscillate by a small angular displacement. The frequency of oscillation of the rod is ${f_1}$ . On the other hand, if both the springs are connected at the midpoint of the rod, as shown in Fig. 2 and the rod is made to oscillate by a small angular displacement, then the frequency of oscillation is ${f_2}$ . Ignoring gravity and assuming motion only in the plane of the diagram, the value of $\frac{{{f_1}}}{{{f_2}}}$ is:
Solution:
\[\frac{{M{L^2}}}{3}\ddot \theta  + K.\frac{\ell }{2}.\theta .\frac{\ell }{2} + K.\ell .\theta .\ell  = 0\]
\[\mathop \theta \limits^{..}  + \frac{{15}}{4}\frac{k}{M}\theta  = 0\]
\[\mathop \theta \limits^{..}  =  - \left( {\frac{{15}}{4}\frac{k}{M}} \right)\theta \]
${\omega _1} = \sqrt {\frac{{15K}}{{4M}}} $

\[\frac{1}{3}M{L^2}\mathop \theta \limits^{..}  + 2K\frac{L}{2}\theta .\frac{L}{2} = 0\]
\[\mathop \theta \limits^{..}  + \frac{3}{2}\frac{K}{M}\theta  = 0\]
\[\mathop \theta \limits^{..}  =  - \frac{3}{2}\frac{K}{M}\theta \]
${\omega _2} = \sqrt {\frac{3}{2}\frac{K}{M}} $
$\frac{{{\omega _1}}}{{{\omega _2}}} = \sqrt {\frac{{15}}{4} \times \frac{2}{3}} = \sqrt {\frac{5}{2}} $

Question: The efficiency of a Carnot engine operating with a hot reservoir kept at a temperature of 1000 K is 0.4. It extracts 150 J of heat per cycle from the hot reservoir. The work extracted from this engine is being fully used to run a heat pump which has a coefficient of performance 10. The hot reservoir of the heat pump is at a temperature of 300 K. Which of the following statements is/are correct:
Options: Work extracted from the Carnot engine in one cycle is 60 J.
Temperature of the cold reservoir of the Carnot engine is 600 K.
Temperature of the cold reservoir of the heat pump is 270 K
Heat supplied to the hot reservoir of the heat pump in one cycle is 540 J.
Solution:
$\eta = 0.4$
${\left( {COP} \right)_{HP}} = 10$
$W = \eta \times {Q_1} = 0.4 \times 150 = 60J$
$COP = \frac{{{\text{Dissered Effect}}}}{{{\text{Work input}}}}$
$10 = \frac{{{Q_3}}}{W}$
${Q_3} = 600J$
Also $\eta = 0.4 = 1 - \frac{{{T_2}}}{{{T_1}}}$
${T_2} = 600K$
$COP = \frac{{{T_3}}}{{{T_3} - {T_4}}} = 10$
$ = \frac{{300}}{{300 - {T_4}}} = 10$
${T_4} = 270K$

Question: Six infinitely large and thin non-conducting sheets are fixed in configurations I and II. As shown in the figure, the sheets carry uniform surface charge densities which are indicated in terms of ${\sigma _0}$. The separation between any two consecutive sheets is $1\mu m.$ The various regions between the sheets are denoted as 1, 2, 3, 4 and 5. If ${\sigma _0} = 9\mu C/{m^2}$, then which of the following statements is/are correct: (Take permittivity of free space ${ \in _0} = 9 \times {10^{ - 12}}F/m$):
Options: In region 4 of the configuration I, the magnitude of the electric field is zero.
In region 3 of the configuration II, the magnitude of the electric field is $\frac{{{\sigma _0}}}{{{ \in _0}}}$
Potential difference between the first and the last sheets of the configuration I is 5 V.
Potential difference between the first and the last sheets of the configuration II is zero.
Solution:
${\left( {{E_4}} \right)_I} = \frac{{{\sigma _0}}}{{2{ \in _0}}}\left[ {1 - 1 + 1 - 1 - 1 + 1} \right] = 0$
${\left( {{V_{{\text{First}}}}} \right)_I} = \frac{{ - {\sigma _0}}}{{2{ \in _0}}}\left[ { - 1 + 2 - 3 + 4 - 5} \right]d$
$ = \frac{{ - {\sigma _0}}}{{2{ \in _0}}}\left[ { - 3} \right]d = \frac{{{\sigma _0}3d}}{{2{ \in _0}}}$
${\left( {{V_{Last}}} \right)_I} = \frac{{ - {\sigma _0}}}{{2{ \in _0}}}\left[ {1 - 2 + 3 - 4 + 5} \right]$
$ = \frac{{{\sigma _0}}}{{2{ \in _0}}}\left[ { - 3d} \right]$
${\left( {{V_{{\text{First}}}} - {V_{{\text{Last}}}}} \right)_I} = \frac{{3{\sigma _0}d}}{{{ \in _0}}}$
$ = \frac{{3 \times 9 \times {{10}^{ - 6}} \times 1 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}}}{{9 \times {{10}^{ - 12}}}} = 3{\text{volt}}$

${\left( {{E_3}} \right)_{II}} = \frac{{{\sigma _0}}}{2}\left[ {\frac{1}{2} - 1 + 1 + 1 - 1 + \frac{1}{2}} \right] = \frac{{{\sigma _0}}}{{2{ \in _0}}}$
$ = \frac{{ - {\sigma _0}}}{{2{ \in _0}}}\left[ { - 1 + 2 - 3 + 4 - \frac{5}{2}} \right]d$
$ = \frac{{ - {\sigma _0}}}{{2{ \in _0}}}\left[ {2 - 2.5} \right]d = \frac{{{\sigma _0}d}}{{4{ \in _0}}}$
${\left( {{V_{Last}}} \right)_{II}} = \frac{{ - {\sigma _0}}}{{2{ \in _0}}}\left[ {1 - 2 + 3 - 4 + \frac{5}{2}} \right]d$
$ = \frac{{ - {\sigma _0}}}{{2{ \in _0}}}\left[ {6.5 - 6} \right]d = \frac{{ - {\sigma _0}d}}{{4{ \in _0}}}$
${\left( {{V_{First}} - {V_{Last}}} \right)_{II}} = \frac{{{\sigma _0}d}}{{2{ \in _0}}} \ne 0$

Question: A hydrogen atom, initially at rest in its ground state, absorbs a photon of frequency ${v_1}$ and ejects the electron with a kinetic energy of 10 eV. The electron then combines with a positron at rest to form a positronium atom in its ground state and simultaneously emits a photon of frequency ${v_2}$. The center of mass of the resulting positronium atom moves with a kinetic energy of 5 eV. It is given that positron has the same mass as that of electron and the positronium atom can be considered as a Bohr atom, in which the electron and the positron orbit around their center of mass. Considering no other energy loss during the whole process, the difference between the two photon energies (in eV) is ____
Solution:
$h{v_1} = 13.6 + 10 = 23.6eV\,\,\,.....\left( 1 \right)$
Energy of positronium in ground state
$ = - 13.6\frac{\mu }{m}{\left( {\frac{z}{n}} \right)^2}eV$
$ = - 13.6 \times \frac{1}{2}eV = - 6.8eV$
So to make positronium 6.8 eV must release & 5 eV is the KE of COM.
So total energy of photon released $\left( {h{v_2}} \right)$ will be:
$h{v_2} = \left( {10 - 5} \right) + 6.8 = 11.8eV\,\,\,\,\,.....\left( 2 \right)$
$\therefore $ Difference in energy $ = 23.6 - 11.8 = 11.8eV$

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Topic: Energy, radius and speed of electron in hydrogen like elements

Question: A conducting solid sphere of radius R and mass M carries a charge Q. The sphere is rotating about an axis passing through its center with a uniform angular speed ω . The ratio of the magnitudes of the magnetic dipole moment to the angular momentum about the same axis is given as $\alpha \frac{Q}{{2M}}$. The value of $\alpha $ is ____.
Solution:
$dM = dIA$
$A = \pi {r^2} = \pi {\left( {R\sin \theta } \right)^2}$
$dI = \frac{{da}}{T} = \frac{{\sigma \left( {2\pi r} \right)\left( {Rd\theta } \right)\omega }}{{2\pi }}$
$dI = \frac{{\sigma 2\pi {R^2}\omega \sin \theta d\theta }}{{2\pi }}$
$dI = \sigma {R^2}\omega \sin \theta d\theta $
Magnetic dipole moment:
$M = \int {dM = \int\limits_0^\pi {\sigma {R^2}\omega \pi {R^2}{{\sin }^3}\theta d\theta } } $
$M = \sigma {R^4}\omega \pi \int\limits_0^\pi {{{\sin }^3}\theta d\theta } \,\,\,\,\,\left( {\because \int\limits_0^\pi {{{\sin }^3}\theta d\theta = \frac{4}{3}} } \right)$
$M = \left( {\frac{Q}{{4\pi {R^2}}}} \right){R^4}\omega \pi \left( {\frac{4}{3}} \right)$
Magnetic dipole moment
$M = \frac{{Q{R^2}\omega }}{3}$
Angular momentum
$L = \left( {\frac{2}{5}M{R^2}} \right)\omega $
$\frac{M}{L} = \frac{{Q{R^2}\omega }}{{3 \times \frac{2}{5}M{R^2}\omega }} = \frac{Q}{{2M}}\left( {\frac{5}{3}} \right)$
$\alpha = \frac{5}{3} = 1.67$

Question: Two identical concave mirrors each of focal length $f$ are facing each other as shown in the schematic diagram. The focal length $f$ is much larger than the size of the mirrors. A glass slab of thickness 𝑡 and refractive index ${n_0}$ is kept equidistant from the mirrors and perpendicular to their common principal axis. A monochromatic point light source S is embedded at the center of the slab on the principal axis, as shown in the schematic diagram. For the image to be formed on S itself, which of the following distances between the two mirrors is/are correct:
Solution:
$\frac{{S - t}}{2} + \frac{t}{{2{n_0}}} = 2f$
$S + t\left( {\frac{1}{{{n_0}}} - 1} \right) = 4f$
$S = 4f + \left( {1 - \frac{1}{{{n_0}}}} \right)t$
Also $\frac{{S - t}}{2} + \frac{t}{{2{n_0}}} = f$
$S = 2f + \left( {1 - \frac{1}{{{n_0}}}} \right)t$

Question: A positive point charge of ${10^{ - 8}}C$ is kept at a distance of 20 cm from the center of a neutral conducting sphere of radius 10 cm. The sphere is then grounded and the charge on the sphere is measured. The grounding is then removed and subsequently the point charge is moved by a distance of 10 cm further away from the center of the sphere along the radial direction. Taking $\frac{1}{{4\pi { \in _0}}} = 9 \times {10^9}N{m^2}/{C^2}$ (where ${ \in _0}$ is the permittivity of free space), which of the following statements is/are correct: :
Options: Before the grounding, the electrostatic potential of the sphere is 450 V.
Charge flowing from the sphere to the ground because of grounding is $5 \times {10^{ - 9}}C$
After the grounding is removed, the charge on the sphere is $ - 5 \times {10^{ - 9}}C$
The final electrostatic potential of the sphere is 300 V.
Solution:
Before grounding
${V_{sphere}} = {\left( {{V_C}} \right)_{net}} = {\left( {{V_C}} \right)_q} + {\left( {{V_C}} \right)_{ind}}$
${V_{sphere}} = \frac{{kq}}{\ell } + 0 = \frac{{9 \times {{10}^9} \times {{10}^{ - 8}}}}{{0.2}} = \frac{{90}}{{0.2}} = \frac{{900}}{2} = 450\,volt$
After grounding

$\frac{{kQ}}{\ell } + \frac{{kqs}}{R} = {V'_{Sphere}} = 0$
${q_s} = - \frac{R}{\ell }q = - \frac{1}{2} \times {10^{ - 8}} = - 5 \times {10^{ - 9}}$
${q_s} = - 5 \times {10^{ - 9}}$Coulomb
Charge flower from sphere to ground $ = 5 \times {10^{ - 9}}$ Coulomb
After grounding is removed
${\left( {{V_{sphere}}} \right)_{final}} = \frac{{kq}}{{\ell '}} + \frac{{k{q_s}}}{R}$
$ = \frac{{9 \times {{10}^9} \times {{10}^2} \times {{10}^{ - 8}}}}{{30}} - \frac{{9 \times {{10}^9} \times 5 \times {{10}^{ - 9}} \times {{10}^2}}}{{10}}$
$ = \frac{{9 \times 1000}}{{30}} - 450 = 300\,volt - 450volt = - 150volt$

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Topic: Electric field and potential problems involving conductor

Question: A geostationary satellite above the equator is orbiting around the earth at a fixed distance ${r_1}$ from the center of the earth. A second satellite is orbiting in the equatorial plane in the opposite direction to  the earth's rotation, at a distance ${r_2}$ from the center of the earth, such that ${r_1} = 1.21{r_2}$. The time period of the second satellite as measured from the geostationary satellite is $\frac{{24}}{p}$hours. The value of p is _____.
Solution:
$T \propto {r^{\frac{3}{2}}}$
$\frac{{{T_2}}}{{{T_1}}} = {\left( {\frac{{{r_2}}}{{{r_1}}}} \right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}$
$\frac{{{\omega _2}}}{{{\omega _1}}} = {\left( {\frac{{{r_1}}}{{{r_2}}}} \right)^{\frac{3}{2}}} = {\left( {1.21} \right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}$
${\omega _2} = {\omega _1}\left( {1.331} \right)\,\,\,......\left( i \right)$
$\left( {{\omega _2} + {\omega _1}} \right){t_0} = 2\pi \,\,\,.....\left( {ii} \right)$
${t_0} = \frac{{2\pi }}{{{\omega _2} + {\omega _1}}} = \frac{{2\pi }}{{\left( {\frac{3}{4} + 1} \right){\omega _1}}} = \frac{{6\pi }}{{7{\omega _1}}}$
${t_0} = \frac{{6\pi }}{{2\pi }}\frac{{\left( {{T_{GSS}}} \right)}}{{\left( 7 \right)}}$
${t_0} = \frac{{3 \times 24\,hours}}{7} = \frac{{24}}{p}hours$
$p = \frac{7}{3} = 2.33$

Question: An ideal monatomic gas of n moles is taken through a cycle WXYZW consisting of consecutive adiabatic and isobaric quasi-static processes, as shown in the schematic V-T diagram. The volume of the gas at W, X and Y points are, 64 cm3, 125 cm3 and 250 cm3, respectively. If the absolute temperature of the gas TW at the point W is such that $nR{T_W} = 1J$ (R is the universal gas constant), then the amount of heat absorbed (in J) by the gas along the path XY is ____
Solution:
$nR{T_w} = {P_w}{V_w} = 1J$
${P_W} = \frac{1}{{64}} \times {10^6}Pa$
For $WX$ process
${P_X}V_X^y = {P_W}V_W^y$
$ \Rightarrow {P_X} = {P_W}{\left( {\frac{{{V_W}}}{{{V_X}}}} \right)^y}$
amount of heat absorbed in XY process
$Q = nCP\Delta T = n \times \frac{5}{2}R \times \left[ {{T_Y} - {T_X}} \right]$ [For monoatomic gas ${C_P} = \frac{{5R}}{2}$]
$Q = \frac{5}{2}\left[ {nR{T_Y} - nR{T_X}} \right]$
$ = \frac{5}{2}\left[ {{P_Y}{V_Y} - {P_X}{V_X}} \right]$
$ = \frac{5}{2}{P_X}\left[ {{V_Y} - {V_X}} \right]$ $\left[ {\because {P_X} = {P_Y};\,{\text{Isobaric process}}} \right]$
$ = \frac{5}{2} \times {P_W} \times {\left[ {\frac{{{V_W}}}{{{V_X}}}} \right]^y}\left[ {{V_Y} - {V_X}} \right]$
Putting values:
$Q = 1.6\,Joule$

Question: In a Young’s double slit experiment, a combination of two glass wedges 𝐴 and 𝐵, having refractive indices 1.7 and 1.5, respectively, are placed in front of the slits, as shown in the figure. The separation between the slits is 𝑑 = 2 mm and the shortest distance between the slits and the screen is 𝐷 = 2 m. Thickness of the combination of the wedges is 𝑡 = 12 𝜇m. The value of 𝑙 as shown in the figure is 1 mm. Neglect any refraction effect at the slanted interface of the wedges. Due to the combination of the wedges, the central maximum shifts (in mm) with respect to O by ____
Solution:
$x + y = 12\mu m$
$\frac{4}{{12}} = \frac{1}{x}$
$x = 3\mu m$
$y = 6\mu m$
$\therefore \Delta = \frac{{yd}}{D} - \left( {{\mu _B} - 1} \right)x - \left( {{\mu _A} - 1} \right)y + \left( {{\mu _B} - 1} \right)y + \left( {{\mu _A} - 1} \right)x$
$\frac{{ - yd}}{D} = - 0.5 \times 3 - 0.7 \times 9 + 0.5 \times 9 + 0.7 \times 3$
$\frac{{ - yd}}{D} = - 0.5 \times 6 - 0.7 \times 6$
$\frac{{ - yd}}{D} = - 1.2\mu m$
$ \Rightarrow y = \frac{{1.2 \times D}}{d} = \frac{{1.2 \times 2}}{{2 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}} \times {10^{ - 6}} = 1.2mm$

Tags.Topic: Variations in Young's double slit experiment

Question: The left and right compartments of a thermally isolated container of length L are separated by a thermally conducting, movable piston of area A. The left and right compartments are filled with $\frac{3}{2}$ and 1 moles of an ideal gas, respectively. In the left compartment the piston is attached by a spring with spring constant k and natural length $\frac{{2L}}{5}.$ In thermodynamic equilibrium, the piston is at a distance $\frac{L}{2}$ from the left and right edges of the container as shown in the figure. Under the above conditions, if the pressure in the right compartment is \[P = \frac{{kL}}{A}\alpha ,\] then the value of $\alpha $ is ____
Solution:
Extension in spring
$x = 0.5L - 0.4L = 0.1L$
FBD of piston

$kx + {P_2}A = {P_1}A$
${P_2}A = {P_1}A - kx$
${P_2} = {P_1} - \frac{{kL}}{{A\left( {10} \right)}}\,\,\,\,...\left( i \right)$
${P_1}V = {n_1}RT$
${P_2}V = {n_2}RT$
$\frac{{{P_1}}}{{{P_2}}} = \frac{{{n_1}}}{{{n_2}}} = \frac{3}{2}$
${P_1} = \frac{3}{2}{P_2}\,\,\,....\left( {ii} \right)$
${P_2} = \frac{3}{2}{P_2} - \frac{{kL}}{{10A}}$
$\frac{{{P_2}}}{2} = \frac{{kL}}{{10A}}$
${P_2} = \frac{{kL}}{{5A}} = \frac{{kL}}{A}\alpha $
$\alpha = \frac{1}{5} = 0.2$

Question: The ratio (in mol $dm^{-3}$) of the coefficient of $V_{m}^{2}$ to the coefficient of $V_{m}$ for a van der Waals gas with $a = 6.0$, $b = 0.060$ at 300 K and 300 atm is
Solution:
Van der Waals equation: $(P + \frac{a}{V_{m}^{2}})(V_{m} - b) = RT$
Rearranging to cubic form: \[PV_m^3 - (Pb + RT)V_m^2 + a{V_m} - ab = 0\]
Coefficient of \[V_m^2 =  - (Pb + RT) =  - (300 \times 0.06 + 0.0821 \times 300) =  - (18 + 24.63) =  - 42.63\]
Coefficient of $V_{m} = a = 6.0$
Ratio = \[\frac{{ - 42.63}}{{6.0}} \approx  - 7.10\].

Question: The monomer (X) involved in Nylon 6,6 synthesis gives positive carbylamine test. If 10 moles of X are analyzed by Dumas method, the amount (in grams) of $N_{2}$ gas evolved is
Solution:
Monomer X is Hexamethylenediamine: \[N{H_2}{(C{H_2})_6}N{H_2}\].
One mole of X contains 2 Nitrogen atoms, which will produce 1 mole of $N_{2}$ gas.
10 moles of X produce 10 moles of \[{N_2}\].

Mass of \[{N_2} = 10 \times 28 = 280g\].

Question: Considering ideal gas behavior, the expansion work done (in kJ) when 144 g of water is electrolyzed completely under constant pressure at 300 K is
Solution:
\[{H_2}O(l) \to {H_2}(g) + \frac{1}{2}{O_2}(g)\]
Moles of water = $144 / 18 = 8$ moles.
\[\Delta {n_g} = 8{\text{ moles }}{H_2} + 4{\text{ moles }}{O_2} = 12\] moles.
$Work = \Delta n_{g}RT = 12 \times 8.314 \times 300 = 29930 J = 29.93 kJ$.
(Using $R=8.3$, $W = 12 \times 8.3 \times 300 = 29.88 kJ$).

Question: The heating of $NH_{4}NO_{2}$ at $60-70^{\circ}C$ and $NH_{4}NO_{3}$ at $200-250^{\circ}C$ is associated with the formation of nitrogen containing compounds X and Y, respectively. X and Y, respectively, are

\[{\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_4}{\text{N}}{{\text{O}}_3}\xrightarrow[{200 - {{250}^{\text{o}}}{\text{C}}}]{\Delta }{{\text{N}}_2}{\text{O}} + 2{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{O}}\]

Question: One of the products formed from the reaction of permanganate ion with iodide ion in neutral aqueous medium is
Solution:
In neutral or faintly alkaline medium, permanganate ion oxidizes iodide to iodate:
\[2MnO_4^ -  + {H_2}O + {I^ - } \to 2Mn{O_2} + 2O{H^ - } + IO_3^ - \]

Question: The correct order of the wavelength maxima of the absorption band in the ultraviolet-visible region for the given complexes is
Solution:
\[{\Delta _0} \propto \frac{1}{\lambda }\]
According to the spectrochemical series, the strength of ligands is \[C{l^ - } < {H_2}O < N{H_3} < C{N^ - }\]. Since $\Delta_{0}$ is inversely proportional to wavelength, the order of $\lambda_{max}$ is: \[{[Co{(CN)_6}]^{3 - }} < {[Co{(N{H_3})_6}]^{3 + }} < {[Co{(N{H_3})_5}{H_2}O]^{3 + }} < {[Co{(N{H_3})_5}Cl]^{2 + }}\].

Question: Regarding the molecular orbital (MO) energy levels for homonuclear diatomic molecules, the INCORRECT statement(s) is(are)
Options: The highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) of $F_{2}$ is $\pi$-type.
Bond energy of $O_{2}^{+}$ is smaller than the bond energy of $O_{2}$.
Bond length of $Li_{2}$ is larger than the bond length of $B_{2}$.
Solution:
A) Ne2 has equal numbers of bonding and antibonding electrons; Bond Order = 0.
B) In F2, the HOMO is the \[\pi *2p\] (antibonding) orbital, not $\pi$-type bonding.
C) \[O_2^ + \] has a bond order of 2.5, while O2 has a bond order of 2.0. Higher bond order means higher bond energy.
D) $Li$ has a larger atomic radius than $B$, so Li2 has a longer bond length.

Question: Consider the depicted hydrogen (H) in the hydrocarbons given below. The most acidic hydrogen (H) is
Solution:
The acidity of a hydrocarbon depends on the stability of its conjugate base.
Removal of H+ from cyclopentadiene (B) results in the cyclopentadienyl anion, which is aromatic ($6\pi$ electrons) and exceptionally stable.

Question:
For the reaction sequence:

The correct statement(s) is(are)
Options: C-X bond length in P, Q and R follows the order $Q > R > P$
C-X bond enthalpy in P, Q and R follows the order $R > P > Q$
Relative reactivity toward $S_{N}2$ reaction in P, Q and R follows the order $P > R > Q$
$pK_{a}$ value of the conjugate acids of the leaving groups in P, Q and R follows the order $R > Q > P$
Solution:
P is an alkyl bromide (C-Br), Q is an alkyl iodide (C-I), and R is an alkyl fluoride (C-F).
Bond Enthalpy: $C-F > C-Cl > C-Br > C-I$. Thus, $R > P > Q$.
Bond Length: $C-I > C-Br > C-F$. Thus, $Q > P > R$.

Question: The pair(s) of diamagnetic ions is(are)
Solution:
La3+ and Ce4+ have [Xe]4f0 configuration (no unpaired electrons).
Yb2+ and Lu3+ have [Xe]4f14 configuration (fully filled, no unpaired electrons).
The other ions have partially filled $f$ or $d$ orbitals and are paramagnetic.

Question: At 25oC the concentration of H+ ions in \[1.00 \times {10^{ - 3}}M\] aqueous solution of a weak monobasic acid having \[{K_a} = 4.00 \times {10^{ - 11}}\] is \[X \times {10^{ - 7}}M\]. The value of \[X\] is
Solution:
Since the acid is extremely weak, we consider the contribution of water ($K_{w}$).
\[[{H^ + }] = \sqrt {C{K_a} + {K_w}}  = \sqrt {({{10}^{ - 3}} \times 4 \times {{10}^{ - 11}}) + {{10}^{ - 14}}} \]
\[[{H^ + }] = \sqrt {4 \times {{10}^{ - 14}} + 1 \times {{10}^{ - 14}}}  = \sqrt {5 \times {{10}^{ - 14}}} \]
\[[{H^ + }] = 2.236 \times {10^{ - 7}}\]. Thus, $X = 2.236$.

Tags.Subject: Chemistry
Tags.Topic: Dissociation of Weak Acids and Bases

Question: In an electrochemical cell, dichromate ions in aqueous acidic medium are reduced to $Cr^{3+}$. The current (in amperes) that flows through the cell for 48.25 minutes to produce 1 mole of $Cr^{3+}$ is
Solution:
Half reaction: \[C{r_2}O_7^{2 - } + 14{H^ + } + 6{e^ - } \to 2C{r^{3 + }} + 7{H_2}O\]
To produce 2 moles of \[C{r^{3 + }}\], 6 moles of e– are needed.
To produce 1 mole of Cr3+, 3 moles of e– are needed (n = 3).
Q = nF = 3 × 96500 Coulombs.
\[Q = I \times t \Rightarrow 3 \times 96500 = I \times (48.25 \times 60)\]
\[I = \frac{{289500}}{{2895}} = 100A.\]

Question: Using the expression\[2d\sin \theta = \lambda ,\] one calculates the values of d by measuring the corresponding angles \[\theta \] in the range 0 to \[{90^ \circ }\]. The wavelength \[\lambda \] is exactly known and the error in \[\theta \] is constant for all values of \[\theta .\] As \[\theta \] increases from \[{0^\circ }\]
Options: the absolute error in d remains constant.
the absolute error in d increases
the fractional error in d remains constant.
the fractional error in d decreases.
Solution:
\[d = \frac{\lambda }{{2\sin \theta }}\]
\[\ln d = \ln \left( {\frac{\lambda }{2}} \right) - \ln \sin \theta \]
\[\frac{{\Delta d}}{d} = 0 - \frac{{\cos \theta \Delta \theta }}{{\sin \theta }}\]
\[{\left( {\frac{{\Delta d}}{d}} \right)_{\max }} = \pm \cot \theta \Delta \theta \]
Also \[{(\Delta d)_{\max }} = d\cot \theta \Delta \theta \]
=\[\frac{\lambda }{{2\sin \theta }}\cot \theta \Delta \theta \]
\[ = \frac{\lambda }{2}\frac{{\cos \theta }}{{{{\sin }^2}\theta }}\Delta \theta \]
As \[\theta \] increases \[\cot \theta \] decreases and \[\frac{{\cos \theta }}{{{{\sin }^2}\theta }}\] also decreases.

Question:
A point Q is moving in a circular orbit of radius R in the x-y plane with an angular velocity $$\omega $$. This can be considered as equivalent to a loop carrying a steady current $$\frac{{Q\omega }}{{2\pi }}$$. A uniform magnetic field along the positive z-axis is now switched on, which increases at a constant rate from 0 to B in one second. Assume that the radius of the orbit remains constant. The application of the magnetic field induces an emf in the orbit. The induced emf is defined as the work done by an induced electric field in moving a unit positive charge around closed loop. It is known that, for an orbiting charge, the magnetic dipole moment is proportional to the angular momentum with a proportionality constant $$\gamma $$.


The magnitude of the induced electric field in the orbit at any instant of time during the time interval of the magnetic field change, is

In the circuit shown in the figure, there are two parallel plate capacitors each of the capacitance C. The switch S1 is pressed first to fully charge the capacitor C1 and then released. The switch S2 is then pressed to charge the capacitor C2. After some time S2 is released and then S3 is pressed. After some time,
Options: the charge on the upper plate of  \[{C_1}\] is \[2C{V_{\text{o}}}\].
the charge on the upper plate of \[{C_1}\] is \[C{V_{\text{o}}}\].
the charge on the upper plate of \[{C_2}\] is 0.
the charge on the upper plate of \[{C_2}\] is \[ - C{V_{\text{o}}}\].
Solution:
For \[{S_1},\] \[{Q_f} = 2C{V_0}\]
For \[{S_2}\]charge exchange between \[{C_1}\]and \[{C_2}\]they has equal capacitance so charge will divided equally
\[{Q_{{f_1}}} = + CV\left( {{\text{Top plate}}} \right),{Q_{{f_2}}} = + CV\left( {{\text{Top plate}}} \right)\]
For \[{S_3}\]
Polarity of \[{C_2}\]will change so finally its top plate will get – CV0 charge so (b) (d).

Question:
Correct statement(s) about the compounds X, Y and Z is (are)
\[{\text{Mn}}{{\text{O}}_2} + {\text{ Conc}}{\text{. HCl}} \to {\text{MnC}}{{\text{l}}_2} + \mathop {\text{X}}\limits_{{\text{ (greenish yellow gas) }}} + {{\text{H}}_2}{\text{O}}\]
\[{\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_3} + \mathop {\text{X}}\limits_{({\text{excess}})} \to {\text{Y}} + {\text{HCl}}\]
\[{\text{X}} + \mathop {{{\text{F}}_2}}\limits_{({\text{excess}})} \xrightarrow{{573\;{\text{K}}}}{\text{Z}}\]
Options: X is used for sterilizing drinking water.
Z is used in the enrichment of <sup>235</sup>U.
Y is a stronger Lewis base than ammonia.
Solution:
\[{\text{Mn}}{{\text{O}}_2} + {\text{conc}}{\text{. HCl}} \to {\text{MnC}}{{\text{l}}_2} + \mathop {{\text{C}}{{\text{l}}_2}}\limits_{{\text{ }}\left( {\text{X}} \right){\text{(greenish yellow gas)}}} + {{\text{H}}_2}{\text{O}}\]
\[{\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_3} + \mathop {{\text{C}}{{\text{l}}_2}}\limits_{({\text{excess}})} \to \mathop {{\text{NC}}{{\text{l}}_3}}\limits_{({\text{Y}})} + {\text{HCl}}\]
\[{\text{C}}{{\text{l}}_2} + \mathop {{{\text{F}}_2}}\limits_{({\text{excess}})} \xrightarrow{{573\;{\text{K}}}}\mathop {{\text{Cl}}{{\text{F}}_3}}\limits_{({\text{Z}})} \]
(A) Cl2 is used for sterilizing drinking water
(B) NCl3 is non-planar as hybridization of nitrogen is sp3 (pyramidal)
(C) ClF3 is used in enrichment of 235U to produce volatile UF6
(U + 3ClF3 → UF6 + 3ClF)

(D) NCl3 is weaker Lewis base than NH3 because the highly electronegative chlorine atoms pull electron density away from the nitrogen (–I effect)

Tags.Subject: Chemistry
Tags.Topic: General Properties and Compounds of Halogens

Question: The 2s and the 2p orbital energies of hydrogen atom are E2s(H) and E2p(H), respectively. The 2s and the 2p orbital energies of lithium atom are E2s(Li) and E2p(Li), respectively. The correct option (s) about the orbital energies is (are)
Solution:
(i) The energy of subshells in multi electron system is compared on the basis of (n + l) rule.
(n + l) value for E2s (Li) = 2
(n + l) value for E2p(Li) = 3
E2s (Li) < E2p(Li)
(ii) In single electron system energy of subshell depends on the value of n
E2s (H) = E2p(H) as n is same.
(iii) For Li, Z = 3 more effective nuclear charge pulling its 2s electron more closer and resulting
more negative lower energy.

Question: Two cylinders, both fitted with frictionless pistons, are filled with mixtures of He and Ar gases. In the first cylinder, the masses of He and Ar are m1 and m2, respectively. In the second cylinder, the masses of He and Ar are m2 and m1, respectively the molar mass of Ar is 10 times the molar mass of He. The external pressure applied by the piston on the first cylinder needs to be 5 times that on the second cylinder so that the volume of the gas mixtures in both the cylinders are equal at the same temperature. Assuming He and Ar behaves like ideal gases, the value of (m1/m2) is _____.
Solution:
Given MAr = 10 MHe MHe = M
 P1 = 5 P2
 V1 = V2 = V
 T1 = T2 = T
Applying ideal gas equation
\[{\text{vessel (I) }} \Rightarrow {P_1}V = \left[ {\frac{{{m_1}}}{M} + \frac{{{m_2}}}{{10M}}} \right]RT\]
\[{\text{vessel (II)}} \Rightarrow {P_2}V = \left[ {\frac{{{m_2}}}{M} + \frac{{{m_1}}}{{10M}}} \right]RT\]
Dividing (II) / (I)
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{{{P_2}}}{{{P_1}}} = \frac{{\frac{{{m_2}}}{M} + \frac{{{m_1}}}{{10M}}}}{{\frac{{{m_1}}}{M} + \frac{{{m_2}}}{{10M}}}} = \frac{1}{5}\]
\[ \Rightarrow 5{m_2} + \frac{{{m_1}}}{2} = {m_1} + \frac{{{m_2}}}{{10}}\]
\[ \Rightarrow 5{m_2} - \frac{{{m_2}}}{{10}} = {m_1} - \frac{{{m_1}}}{2}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{{49{m_2}}}{{10}} = \frac{{{m_1}}}{2} \Rightarrow \frac{{{m_1}}}{{{m_2}}} = 9.8\]
Ans. 9.80

Question:
Reaction of PtF6 with oxygen (O2) gas results in the formation of an ionic compound, X+Y–.
Correct statement(s) is (are)
Options: Valence d-orbitals of the metal ion in X<sup>+</sup>Y<sup>– </sup>has 5 electrons.
PtF<sub>6</sub> acts as an oxidant in this reaction.
PtF<sub>6 </sub>acts as a fluorinating agent in this reaction.
Solution:
PtF6 + O2 → O2[PtF6]
X+ = O2+
Y– = [PtF6] –1
(A) Bond order of O2+ is 2.5
(B) Electronic configuration of Pt is [Xe]4f14 5d9 6s1
[PtF6]–1⇒ Pt+5:- 4f14 5d5
5 electron in valence d orbital
(C) PtF6 oxidizes O2 into O2+ hence act as oxidant
(D) In this reaction fluorination does not take place

Question: An ideal gas (0.5 mol), initially at 2 bar pressure, is compressed at a constant temperature of 600 K in two steps : first against a constant external pressure of P bar (2 < P < 8), and then against constant external pressure of 8 bar. At each step, the compression is stopped only when the pressure of the gas becomes equal to the external pressure. The total work done on the gas in these steps is W. Considering all possible values of P (2 < P < 8 ) and taking the gas constant as R (in J K–1 mol–1), the minimum value of |W| (in J)
Solution:
\[{\text{n}} = 0.5\]

\[{\text{W}} = {{\text{W}}_1} + {{\text{W}}_2}\]
\[ = - 0.5{\text{R }}600\left[ {1 - \frac{{\text{P}}}{2}} \right] - 0.5{\text{R }}600\left[ {1 - \frac{8}{{\text{P}}}} \right]\]
\[{\text{W}} = - 300{\text{R}}\left[ {2 - \frac{{\text{P}}}{2} - \frac{8}{{\text{P}}}} \right]\]
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{{{\text{dW}}}}{{{\text{dP}}}} = - 300{\text{R}}\left[ { - \frac{1}{2} + \frac{8}{{{{\text{P}}^2}}}} \right] = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} = \frac{8}{{{{\text{P}}^2}}} \Rightarrow {\text{P}} = 4{\text{ bar}}\]
\[{\text{W}} = - 300\,{\text{R}}\left[ {2 - \frac{4}{2} - \frac{8}{4}} \right]\]
\[ = - 300{\text{R}}[2 - 2 - 2]\]
\[ = 600\,{\text{R}} = {{\text{W}}_{\min }}\]
\[{\text{Ans}} = {\text{B}}\]

Question:
List-I contains various physical/chemical processes, and List-II contains combinations of changes in enthalpy (∆H) and entropy (∆S). Match each entry in List-I to appropriate entry in List-II, and choose the correct option.
List-I List-II
(P)Physisorption(1)∆H > 0 and ∆S > 0
(Q)Diamond → Graphite (2)∆H < 0 and ∆S < 0
(R)Denaturation of protein (3)∆H < 0 and ∆S = 0
(S)Propene → Cyclopropane (4)∆H > 0 and ∆S < 0
(5)∆H < 0 and ∆S > 0
Solution:
(P) Physisorption ⇒ x(g) → x(s)
⇒ ∆S < 0, ∆H < 0
 (Q) Diamond → Graphite
⇒ ∆S > 0, ∆H < 0
 Graphite has weaker Van Der Waal forces of attraction between layers & hence, greater disorder & graphite is more stable in normal condition.
(R) Denaturation of protein
∆H > 0 [Heat of absorption]
∆S > 0 [relative bonds between polymer protein chain break]
(S) CH3CH = CH2(g) → (g)
∆H > 0 [Cyclopropane is unstable due to angular strain]
∆S > 0 [ open chain has more freedom of rotation]
⇒ P → 2; Q → 5; R → 1; S → 4

Question: The sum of total number of carbonyl groups (>C=O) present in the major products X and Y in the following reactions is _______.

As shown in the figure, five Carnot engines, each with efficiency $\eta $ and same number of cycles per unit time, are operating between six heat reservoirs. The amount of heat released per cycle by one engine is completely absorbed by the next engine. Consider ${{\text{Q}}_0}$ to be the amount of heat absorbed per cycle by the first engine and $W$ as the amount of total work done by all the engines per cycle, then the net efficiency of the system is found to be ${\eta _{{\text{net}}}} = \frac{{\text{W}}}{{{{\text{Q}}_0}}} = \frac{{211}}{{243}}$. The value of $\eta $ is:
Solution:
From the given information
${{\text{W}}_{\text{n}}} = \eta {{\text{Q}}_{{\text{n}} - 1}}$
${{\text{Q}}_{\text{n}}} = {{\text{Q}}_{{\text{n}} - 1}} - {{\text{W}}_{\text{n}}} = {{\text{Q}}_{{\text{n}} - 1}}\left( {1 - \eta } \right)$
$\therefore {{\text{Q}}_1} = {{\text{Q}}_0}\left( {1 - \eta } \right)$
${Q_2} = {Q_1}\left( {1 - \eta } \right) = {Q_0}{(1 - \eta )^2}$
${{\text{Q}}_5} = {{\text{Q}}_0}{(1 - \eta )^5}$
∴ Total work $ = {{\text{Q}}_0} - {{\text{Q}}_5} = {{\text{Q}}_0}\left[ {1 - {{(1 - \eta )}^5}} \right]$
$\therefore {\eta _{{\text{net}}}} = \frac{{{{\text{Q}}_0} - {{\text{Q}}_5}}}{{{{\text{Q}}_0}}} = 1 - {(1 - \eta )^5} = \frac{{211}}{{243}}$
$\therefore {(1 - \eta )^5} = 1 - \frac{{211}}{{243}} = \frac{{32}}{{243}}$
$\therefore 1 - \eta = \frac{2}{3} \Rightarrow \eta = \frac{1}{3} = 0.33$

Question: As shown in the figure, an insulated container is fitted with a thermally conducting but immovable partition ( ${P_1}$ ) and a freely movable but thermally insulated piston ( ${P_2}$ ). The partition ${P_1}$ with thermal conductivity $K$, cross sectional area A and width $x$ divides the container into two sections, ${S_1}$ and ${S_2}$, each containing one mole of a monoatomic gas. The piston ${P_2}$ moves freely such that the gas in ${S_2}$ is always at the atmospheric pressure. Initially, the difference between the temperatures of ${S_1}$ and ${S_2}$ is $$\Delta {T_0}$$. The time it takes for the temperature difference to become $$\frac{{\Delta {T_0}}}{2}$$ is $nxR/KA$, where $R$ is the universal gas constant. The value of $n$ is: [Given: ${\text{ln}}2 \approx 0.7$ ]
Solution:
Let at any instant temperature difference be $\left( {{{\text{T}}_{{\text{S}}1}} - {{\text{T}}_{{\text{S}}2}}} \right)$
$\therefore \frac{{{\text{dQ}}}}{{{\text{dt}}}} = \frac{{{\text{KA}}\left( {{{\text{T}}_{{\text{s}}1}} - {{\text{T}}_{{\text{s}}2}}} \right)}}{{\text{x}}}$
Since ${{\text{V}}_{{\text{s}}1}} = $ constant $ \Rightarrow {\text{dQ}} = {\text{n}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{V}}}{\text{d}}{{\text{T}}_1} = \frac{3}{2}{\text{Rd}}{{\text{T}}_1}$
Since ${{\text{P}}_{{\text{S}}2}} = $ constant $ \Rightarrow {\text{dQ}} = {\text{n}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{P}}}{\text{d}}{{\text{T}}_2} = \frac{5}{2}{\text{Rd}}{{\text{T}}_2}$
$$\therefore \frac{{d\left( {\Delta T} \right)}}{{dt}} = \frac{{d{T_1}}}{{dt}} - \frac{{d{T_2}}}{{dt}} =  - \frac{{dQ/dt}}{{\frac{3}{2}R}} - \frac{{dQ/dt}}{{\frac{5}{2}R}}$$
$$\therefore  - \frac{{kA\Delta T}}{{xR}}\left( {\frac{2}{3} + \frac{2}{5}} \right) = \frac{{d\left( {\Delta T} \right)}}{{dt}}$$
$$ \Rightarrow \frac{{d\left( {\Delta T} \right)}}{{dt}} =  - \frac{{kA\Delta T}}{{xR}} \times \frac{{16}}{{15}}$$
$ \Rightarrow \ell {\text{n}}2 = \frac{{16}}{{15}}\frac{{{\text{KA}}}}{{{\text{xR}}}}{\text{t}}$
$\therefore {\text{t}} = \frac{{0.7 \times 15 \times {\text{R}}}}{{16{\text{KA}}}}$
$\therefore {\text{n}} = 0.65625 \approx 0.66$

Question:
The electric field associated with an electromagnetic wave travelling in vacuum is given by ${E_0}\sin \left( {3y + 4z + \omega t} \right)\hat i$, where $\omega $ is the angular frequency. All quantities are in SI units. The correct statement(s) about this wave is/are:
[Given: speed of light in vacuum $c = 3 \times {10^8}{\text{m}}{{\text{s}}^{ - 1}}$.]
Options: The wave is travelling in $ - \frac{1}{5}\left( {3\hat j + 4\hat k} \right)$ direction.
The magnitude of the wave vector is $0.5{\text{}}{{\text{m}}^{ - 1}}$.
The magnetic field associated with this wave is given by $\frac{{{E_0}}}{c}\sin \left( {3y + 4z + \omega t} \right)\left( {4\hat j - 3\hat k} \right)$
Solution:
$\vec E = {E_0}\sin \left( {\omega t + \left( {x\hat i + y\hat j + z\hat k} \right) \cdot 5\left( {\frac{{3\hat j}}{5} + \frac{{4\hat k}}{5}} \right)} \right)\hat i$
⇒ Wave propagating along $\hat v = - \left( {\frac{{3\hat j}}{5} + \frac{{4\hat k}}{5}} \right)$
$ \Rightarrow {\text{}}\left( {\text{A}} \right)$ is correct
(B) Wave vector is $ - 5\left( {\frac{{3\hat j}}{5} + \frac{{4\hat k}}{5}} \right) \Rightarrow $ its magnitude is $5{\text{}}{{\text{m}}^{ - 1}}$
$ \Rightarrow {\text{}}\left( {\text{B}} \right)$ is wrong
(C) ${\text{C}} = \frac{\omega }{{\text{k}}} \Rightarrow \omega = {\text{ck}} = 15 \times {10^8}{\text{rad}}/{\text{s}}$
$ \Rightarrow {\text{}}\left( {\text{C}} \right)$ is correct
(D) \[\;\hat B = \hat v \times \hat E = - \left( {\frac{{3\hat j}}{5} + \frac{{4\hat k}}{5}} \right) \times \hat i = - \frac{1}{5}\left( { - 3\hat k + 4\hat j} \right) = \frac{{3\hat k}}{5} - \frac{{4\hat j}}{5}\]
$\therefore {\text{}}\vec B = \frac{{{E_0}}}{{5C}}{\text{sin}}\left( {3y + 4z + \omega t} \right)\left( {3\hat k - 4\hat j} \right)$
$ \Rightarrow {\text{}}\left( {\text{D}} \right)$ is wrong

Question: A tank contains two immiscible liquids of densities $6\rho $ and $2\rho $. The higher density liquid is filled up to a height $L/2$ from the bottom. A thin rod of density $\rho $ and length $L$ is fully immersed and hinged at the bottom so that it can oscillate freely, as shown in the figure. If the rod is slightly disturbed from its equilibrium, the time period of small oscillations is $\frac{{2\pi }}{n}\sqrt {\frac{L}{g}} $, where $g$ is the acceleration due to gravity. The value of $n$ is:
Solution:
$${\tau _{{\text{about}}\;{\text{hinge}}}} = {F_{B1}}\frac{{3\;L}}{4}\sin \theta  + {F_{B2}}\frac{{\;L}}{4}\sin \theta  - mg\frac{{\;L}}{2}\sin \theta $$
$${F_{B1}} \approx 2\rho \frac{{\;L}}{2}Ag$$
$${F_{B2}} \approx 6\rho \frac{{\;L}}{2}Ag\left[ {{\text{if}}\,\theta \;{\text{is}}\;{\text{small}}} \right]$$
${\text{m}} = \rho {\text{LA}}$
$$\therefore \tau  = \left[ {\rho \ell Ag\frac{{3\;L}}{4} + 3\rho LAg\frac{{\;L}}{4} - \rho LAg\frac{{\;L}}{2}} \right]\theta $$
$$ = \rho Ag{L^2}\left[ {\frac{3}{4} + \frac{3}{4} - \frac{1}{2}} \right]\theta  = \rho Ag{L^2}\theta $$
$$I = \frac{{m{L^2}}}{3} = \frac{{\rho LA{L^2}}}{3}$$
Since $\tau $ is restoring
$$\therefore \alpha  =  - \frac{{3\rho Ag{L^2}}}{{\rho A{L^3}}}\theta  = \frac{{ - 3\;g}}{{\;L}}\theta $$
$$\therefore T = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{{\;L}}{{3\;g}}} $$
$$\therefore n = \sqrt 3  = 1.732$$ Ans.

Question: A hollow, right circular cone of base radius $R$ and height $h$, with its tip at the origin is rotating about the Z -axis with an angular velocity $\omega $, as shown in the figure. The cone carries a total charge Q uniformly distributed on its curved surface. The magnitude of magnetic field at a point $\left( {0,0,z} \right)$, where $z \gg R$ and $z \gg h$, is $\frac{{n{\mu _0}}}{{4\pi }}\frac{{Q{R^2}\omega }}{{{z^3}}}$. The value of $n$ is:
Solution:
The system can be treated as a magnetic dipole.
$\therefore \frac{M}{L} = \frac{q}{{2m}}$
$\therefore M = \frac{q}{{2m}}L = \frac{Q}{{2m}} \cdot \frac{1}{2}m{R^2}\omega = \frac{{Q\omega {R^2}}}{4}$
$\;\therefore B = 2\frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{4\pi }}\frac{M}{{{r^3}}} = \frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{2\pi }} \cdot \frac{{Q\omega {R^2}}}{{4 \cdot {z^3}}} = \frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{8\pi }}\frac{{Q{R^2}\omega }}{{{z^3}}}$
$\therefore n = 0.5$

Question:
A quasi-static cycle of a monoatomic ideal gas contains an isothermal process (ab), followed by an isochoric process ( $bc$ ) and an adiabatic process ( $ca$ ) as shown in the figure. The volumes of the gas are ${V_1}$ and ${V_2}$ at $a$ and $b$, respectively. If the cycle has heat input ${Q_{{\text{in}}}}$ and output ${Q_{{\text{out}}}}$, then the efficiency of the cycle is defined as $\eta = \frac{{{Q_{{\text{in}}}} - {Q_{{\text{out}}}}}}{{{Q_{{\text{in}}}}}}$. The correct statement(s) is/are:
[Given : ${\text{ln}}2 \approx 0.7$ ]
Options: If ${V_2}/{V_1} = 8$, the heat released in the process $bc$ is smaller than the heat absorbed in the process $ab$.
For a given value of ${V_2}/{V_1},\eta $ does not depend on the temperature of the isothermal process
If ${V_2}/{V_1} = 8$, then the temperature of the gas at $a$ is 4 times the temperature of the gas at $c$.
If ${V_2}/{V_1} = 8$, then the pressure of the gas at $a$ is 4 times the pressure of the gas at $b$.
Solution:
${{\text{C}}_{\text{v}}} = \frac{{3{\text{R}}}}{2},{{\text{C}}_{\text{p}}} = \frac{{5{\text{R}}}}{2},\gamma = \frac{5}{3}$
$${Q_{in}} = {Q_{ab}} = nR{T_a}\ell n\left( {\frac{{{V_2}}}{{\;{V_1}}}} \right)$$
$${Q_{out\;}} =  - {Q_{bc}} =  - n{C_v}\left( {{T_c} - {T_b}} \right) = n{C_v}\left( {{T_a} - {T_c}} \right)$$
also, $$T{V^{\gamma  - 1}} = $$ constant for ca
$ \Rightarrow {{\text{T}}_{\text{a}}}{\text{V}}_1^{2/3} = {{\text{T}}_{\text{c}}}{\text{V}}_2^{2/3}$
(A) $${Q_{ab}} + {Q_{bc}} + {Q_{ca}} > 0$$ (Clockwise P-V cycle)
${{\text{Q}}_{{\text{ab}}}} + {{\text{Q}}_{{\text{bc}}}} + 0 > 0$
$${Q_{ab}} >  - {Q_{bc}}\; \Rightarrow \;\left( A \right)$$ is correct
(B) $$\eta  = \frac{{{Q_{in\;}} - {Q_{out\;}}}}{{{Q_{in\;}}}} = 1 - \frac{{{Q_{out\;}}}}{{{Q_{in\;}}}}$$
$ = 1 - \frac{{{\text{n}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{v}}}\left( {{{\text{T}}_{\text{a}}} - {{\text{T}}_{\text{c}}}} \right)}}{{{\text{nR}}{{\text{T}}_{\text{a}}}{\text{ln}}\left( {\frac{{{{\text{V}}_2}}}{{{\text{}}{{\text{V}}_1}}}} \right)}}$
$ = 1 - \frac{{{{\text{C}}_{\text{v}}}}}{{\text{R}}} \cdot \left( {\frac{{\left( {1 - {{\text{T}}_{\text{c}}}/{{\text{T}}_{\text{a}}}} \right)}}{{{\text{ln}}\left( {{{\text{V}}_2}/{{\text{V}}_1}} \right)}}} \right)$
$ = 1 - \frac{{{{\text{C}}_{\text{v}}}}}{{\text{R}}}\left( {\frac{{1 - {{\left( {{{\text{V}}_1}/{{\text{V}}_2}} \right)}^{2/3}}}}{{\ell {\text{n}}\left( {\frac{{{{\text{V}}_2}}}{{{\text{}}{{\text{V}}_1}}}} \right)}}} \right)$
⇒ Independent of ${{\text{T}}_{\text{a}}} \Rightarrow \left( {\text{B}} \right)$ is correct
(C) $\frac{{{{\text{V}}_2}}}{{{\text{}}{{\text{V}}_1}}} = 8 \Rightarrow \frac{{{\text{}}{{\text{T}}_{\text{a}}}}}{{{{\text{T}}_{\text{c}}}}} = {8^{2/3}} = 4$
$ \Rightarrow {\text{}}\left( {\text{C}} \right)$ is correct
(D) ${\text{}}{P_a}{V_a} = {P_b}{V_b}$
$ \Rightarrow {\text{}}\frac{{{{\text{P}}_{\text{a}}}}}{{{{\text{P}}_{\text{b}}}}} = \frac{{{{\text{V}}_{\text{b}}}}}{{{{\text{V}}_{\text{a}}}}} = \frac{{{{\text{V}}_2}}}{{{\text{}}{{\text{V}}_1}}} = 8 \ne 4$
$ \Rightarrow {\text{}}\left( {\text{D}} \right)$ is wrong

Question:
Consider a large disk of radius R and two smaller disks, each of radius ${\text{r}} = {\text{R}}/50$, lying on its circumference, as shown in the figure. The smaller disks are initially in contact with each other, with an angular separation $$\Delta \theta $$ between their centers. They are made to roll without slipping in opposite directions, with constant angular velocities $\omega $ and $2\omega $ while the large disk is held stationary. The time $\tau $ at which the smaller disks are again in contact is :
[Use $$\sin \left( {\Delta \theta } \right) = \Delta \theta $$ and ignore gravity.]
Solution:
$$\frac{2}{3}\left( {\pi  - \Delta \theta } \right)\left( {R + \frac{R}{{50}}} \right) = \omega t \cdot \frac{R}{{50}}$$
$$\frac{{\Delta \theta }}{2} = \frac{{R/50}}{{R + \frac{R}{{50}}}} = \frac{1}{{51}}$$
$$\Delta \theta  = \frac{2}{{51}}{\text{rad}}$$
$$t = \frac{{102}}{{3\omega }}\left( {\pi  - \Delta \theta } \right)$$
$${\text{t}} = \frac{{102}}{{3\omega }}\left[ {\pi  - \frac{2}{{51}}} \right]$$
${\text{t}} = \frac{{51}}{{3\omega }}\left( {2\pi - \frac{4}{{51}}} \right){\text{sec}}$

Question: Consider a circuit consisting of a capacitor of capacitance $C$ and a coil with $N$ turns per unit length, cross sectional area $S$ and length $d$, where ${d^2} \gg S$. There is another coil of length $d/2$, cross sectional area $S/2$ and $2N$ turns per unit length completely inside the larger coil, as shown in the figure. The ends of this smaller coil are connected with each other by an insulated conducting wire. The self-inductance of the larger coil is $L$. Neglecting edge effects and all the Ohmic resistances, the resonant frequency of the circuit is:
Solution:
Larger coil, $L = {\mu _0}\pi {R^2}{N^2}\ell = {\mu _0}S{N^2}d$
Smaller coil, ${\text{L'}} = {\mu _0}\frac{{{\text{S}}}}{2}{(2{\text{N}})^2}\frac{{{\text{d}}}}{2} = {\text{L}}$
For mutual inductance $\left( {{\mu _0}{\text{Ni}}} \right) \times \frac{{\text{S}}}{2}\left( {2{\text{N}}} \right)\frac{{\text{d}}}{2} = {\text{Mi}}$
${\text{M}} = \frac{{{\mu _0}{\text{S}}{{\text{N}}^2}{\text{d}}}}{2} = \frac{{\text{L}}}{2}$

Induced emf in bigger coil ${\text{e}} = - {\text{L}}\frac{{{\text{di}}}}{{{\text{dt}}}}$
In smaller coil $e' = - M\frac{{di}}{{dt}} - \frac{L}{2}\frac{{di}}{{dt}} = - L\frac{{di'}}{{dt}}$
$\begin{gathered}
  \frac{{{\text{di'}}}}{{{\text{dt}}}} = \frac{1}{2}\frac{{{\text{di}}}}{{{\text{dt}}}} \\
  {\text{i'}} = \frac{{\text{i}}}{2} \\
\end{gathered} $
Net emf in bigger coil
$\begin{gathered}
  e = {e_1} - {e_2} = - L\frac{{di}}{{dt}} + M\frac{{di}}{{dt}} \\
   = - L\frac{{di}}{{dt}} - \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{2}\frac{{di}}{{dt}} \\
   = - \frac{3}{4}\;L\frac{{di}}{{dt}} \\
  \therefore {L_{eq}} = \frac{{3\;L}}{4} \\
  \omega = \frac{1}{{\sqrt {LC} }} = \frac{1}{{\sqrt {\frac{{3\;L}}{4} \cdot C} }} = \frac{2}{{\sqrt {3LC} }} \\
\end{gathered} $

Question:
List-I shows four planar structures made of uniform solid rods each of mass $m$ and length $l$. In the List-II the possible moment of inertia of these structures about an axis $$OCO'$$, which lies in the plane of the structures, are given.
Choose the option that describes the correct match between the entries in List-I to those in List-II.
List – I List – II
(P)(1)$$\frac{5}{4}\;m{\ell ^2}$$
(Q)(2)$$\frac{1}{6}\;m{\ell ^2}$$
(R)(3)$$\frac{1}{{12}}\;m{\ell ^2}$$
(S)(4)$$\frac{2}{3}m{\ell ^2}$$
(5)$$\frac{1}{3}\;m{\ell ^2}$$
Solution:
(P)

$I = \frac{{M{\ell ^2}}}{3}{\sin ^2}45^\circ + \frac{{M{\ell ^2}}}{3}{\sin ^2}45^\circ $$ = \frac{{{\text{M}}{\ell ^2}}}{3}$
(Q)
$I = \frac{{M{\ell ^2}}}{3}{\sin ^2}60^\circ + \frac{{M{\ell ^2}}}{3}{\sin ^2}60^\circ $$ + \left( {0 + {\text{M}}{{\left( {\frac{{\ell \sqrt 3 }}{2}} \right)}^2}} \right)$
${\text{I}} = \frac{{{\text{M}}{\ell ^2}}}{2} + \frac{3}{4}{\text{M}}{\ell ^2} = \frac{5}{4}{\text{M}}{\ell ^2}$
(R)
$I = 4\left( {\frac{{M{\ell ^2}}}{3}{{\sin }^2}45^\circ } \right)$$ = \frac{2}{3}{\text{M}}{\ell ^2}$
(S)
$I = 2 \times \left( {\frac{{M{\ell ^2}}}{3}{{\sin }^2}30^\circ } \right)$$ = \frac{{{\text{M}}{\ell ^2}}}{6}$

Question: Consider a hydrogen atom with ${{\text{v}}_{\text{k}}},{{\text{r}}_{\text{k}}}$ and ${{\text{K}}_{\text{k}}}$ denoting the velocity, orbital radius and kinetic energy of the electron in the ${k^{{\text{th}}}}$ orbit, respectively. The electron undergoes a transition from the ${n^{{\text{th}}}}$ orbit, emitting radiation corresponding to the Lyman series. Considering $h$ to be the Planck's constant and the permittivity of the free space, the correct statement(s) is/are:
Options: Magnitude of change in kinetic energy of electron can be expressed as $\frac{{\text{h}}}{{4\pi }}\left| {\frac{{{\text{n}}{{\text{v}}_{\text{n}}}}}{{{{\text{r}}_{\text{n}}}}} - \frac{{{{\text{v}}_1}}}{{{{\text{r}}_1}}}} \right|$.
Magnitude of change in de Broglie wavelength of the electron can be expressed as .
Frequency of the radiation emitted can be expressed as .

Magnitude of change in total energy of the electron can be expressed as $\frac{{\text{h}}}{{2\pi }}\left| {\frac{{{{\text{v}}_1}}}{{{{\text{r}}_1}}} - \frac{{{\text{n}}{{\text{v}}_{\text{n}}}}}{{{{\text{r}}_{\text{n}}}}}} \right|$.
Solution:
${\text{}}\frac{{{\text{mvv}}_{\text{k}}^2}}{{{{\text{r}}_{\text{k}}}}} = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}} \cdot \frac{{{{\text{e}}^2}}}{{{\text{r}}_{\text{k}}^2}}$
or, ${\text{mv}}_{\text{k}}^2{{\text{r}}_{\text{k}}} = \frac{{{{\text{e}}^2}}}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}$
$\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {m{v_k}{r_k} = \frac{{kh}}{{2\pi }}......\left( 1 \right)}
\end{array}$
$\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\left( 1 \right)/\left( 2 \right) \Rightarrow {v_k} = \frac{{{e^2}}}{{2kh{\varepsilon _0}}}.....\left( 2 \right)}
\end{array}$
${r_k} = \frac{{{\varepsilon _0}{k^2}\;{h^2}}}{{\pi {e^2}\;m}}$
${k_k} = \frac{1}{2}m{v_k}{\;^2} = \frac{1}{2}\;m\frac{{{e^4}}}{{4{k^2}\;{h^2}{\varepsilon _0}{\;^2}}} = \frac{{m{e^4}}}{{8{k^2}\;{h^2}{\varepsilon _0}{\;^2}}} = \frac{{{e^2}}}{{8\pi {\varepsilon _0}{r_k}}}$
${\text{K}} = {\text{n}}$ to 1 for Lymen series
$\frac{h}{{4\pi }}\left| {\frac{{n{v_n}}}{{{r_n}}} - \frac{{{v_1}}}{{{r_1}}}} \right| = \left| {\frac{{nh}}{{2\pi }} \cdot \frac{{{v_n}}}{{2{r_n}}} - \frac{h}{{2\pi }}\frac{{{v_1}}}{{2{r_1}}}} \right|$
$ = \left| {m{v_n}{r_n}\frac{{{v_n}}}{{2{r_n}}} - m{v_1}{r_1}\frac{{{v_1}}}{{2{r_1}}}} \right|$
$ = \left| {\frac{1}{2}mv_n^2 - \frac{1}{2}mv_1^2} \right| = \Delta KE \Rightarrow $ (A) is correct
$\frac{{{e^2}}}{{4{\varepsilon _0}}}\left| {\frac{1}{{{k_n}}} - \frac{1}{{{k_1}}}} \right| = \frac{{{e^2}}}{{4{\varepsilon _0}}}\left| {\frac{2}{{mv_n^2}} - \frac{2}{{mv_1^2}}} \right|$
$ = \frac{{m{e^2}}}{{2{\varepsilon _0}}}\left| {\frac{1}{{\lambda _n^2}} - \frac{1}{{\lambda _1^2}}} \right| \ne \left| {{\lambda _n} - {\lambda _1}} \right|$ (Here ${\lambda _{\text{n}}}$ indicates de-Broglie wave - length)
⇒ (B) is wrong
Frequency $f$ of radiation emitted is given by
${{\text{h}}_f} = {{\text{K}}_1} - {{\text{K}}_{\text{n}}}$
$f = \frac{{{K_1}}}{{\;h}} - \frac{{{K_n}}}{h} = \frac{{{e^2}}}{{8\pi {\varepsilon _0}h{r_1}}} - \frac{{{e^2}}}{{8\pi {\varepsilon _0}h{r_n}}}$
$ = \frac{{{e^2}}}{{8\pi {\varepsilon _0}\;h}}\left( {\frac{1}{{{r_1}}} - \frac{1}{{{r_n}}}} \right) \Rightarrow $ (C) correct
Change in total energy $ = {K_1} - {K_n} = \frac{{{e^2}}}{{8\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\left( {\frac{1}{{{r_1}}} - \frac{1}{{{r_2}}}} \right)$
also, $\frac{h}{{2\pi }}\left| {\frac{{{v_1}}}{{{r_1}}} - \frac{{n{v_n}}}{{{r_4}}}} \right| = \frac{h}{{2\pi }}\left| {{v_1}\frac{{m{v_1}}}{{\;h}}2\pi - n{v_n}\frac{{m{v_n}}}{{nh}}2\pi } \right|$
$ = \left| {mv_1^2 - mv_2^2} \right| = \frac{{{K_1} - {K_n}}}{2} \Rightarrow $ (D) is wrong.

Question: A particle is thrown with a speed $v$ from a point $O$ at an angle $\theta $ with the horizontal plane such that it passes through the point $P$ at a height of 1 m and horizontal distance of 5 m from $O$, as shown in the figure. If acceleration due to gravity is $g{\text{m}}{{\text{s}}^{ - 2}}$, then the correct statement (s) is/are :
Options: If $\theta  = 45^\circ $, the particle reaches its maximum height before it reaches $P$.
If $\theta  = 30^\circ $, the particle reaches its maximum height after reaching $P$.
Solution:
$y = x\tan \theta - \frac{1}{2}\frac{{g{x^2}}}{{{v^2}{{\cos }^2}\theta }}$
Passes through $\left( {5,1} \right)$
$$ \Rightarrow \;1 = 5 \times 1 - \frac{{25\;g}}{{2{v^2}{{\cos }^2}\theta }}$$
(A) If $\theta = 45^\circ $, then
$1 = 5 \times 1 - \frac{{25\;g}}{{2{v^2}{{\left( {\frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}} \right)}^2}}} \Rightarrow \frac{{25\;g}}{{{v^2}}} = 4$
${v^2} = \frac{{25\;g}}{4}$
$v = \frac{{5\sqrt {\;g} }}{2}\;m/s \Rightarrow \left( {\;A} \right)\;{\text{is correct}}.$
(B) If $\theta = {45^ \circ }$
$R = \frac{{{v^2}\sin 90^\circ }}{g} = \frac{{25\;g}}{{4\;g}} = 6.25\;m$
$$\frac{R}{2} = 3.125\;m < 5\;m$$
Hence, particle reaches maximum height before reaching $P$
⇒ (B) is correct
(C) If $\theta = 30^\circ $,
$y = x{\text{tan}}\theta \left( {1 - \frac{x}{R}} \right)$
$1 = \frac{5}{{\sqrt 3 }}\left( {1 - \frac{5}{R}} \right) \Rightarrow \frac{5}{R} = 1 - \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{5}$
$ \Rightarrow R = \frac{{25}}{{5 - \sqrt 3 }} \Rightarrow \frac{R}{2} = \frac{{12.5}}{{5 - \sqrt 3 }} = 3.83 < 5$
⇒ Particle reaches maximum height before reaching $P$.
⇒ (C) is wrong.
(D) If $\theta = {\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{1}{5}} \right) \Rightarrow \tan \theta = \frac{1}{5},\cos \theta = \frac{5}{{\sqrt {26} }}$
$\therefore 1 = 5 \times \frac{1}{5} - \frac{{g \times {5^2}}}{{2{v^2} \times {{\left( {\frac{5}{{\sqrt {26} }}} \right)}^2}}} \Rightarrow v \to \infty \Rightarrow $ (D) incorrect.

Question: A solid cylinder of radius $R$ rolls without slipping with a center of mass speed ${v_0} = \sqrt {\frac{{gR}}{3}} $ on a horizontal surface with a vertical edge, as shown in the figure. Here, $g$ is the acceleration due to the gravity. At the moment when the cylinder loses contact with the surface due to rotation around the corner, the speed of its center of mass is:
Solution:
${\omega _0} = \sqrt {{\text{g}}/3{\text{R}}} $ to loose contact
${\text{m}}{\omega ^2}{\text{R}} = {\text{mgcos}}\theta $
${\omega ^2}{\text{R}} = {\text{gcos}}\theta $
W.E.T
$\begin{gathered}
  mgR\left( {1 - {\text{cos}}\theta } \right) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{3}{2}m{R^2}\left( {{\omega ^2} - \omega _0^2} \right) \\
  g - g{\text{cos}}\theta = \frac{3}{4}R\left( {{\omega ^2} - \frac{g}{{3R}}} \right) \\
  g - {\omega ^2}R = \frac{3}{4}R{\omega ^2} - \frac{g}{4} \\
  \frac{{5g}}{4} = \frac{7}{4}R{\omega ^2} \\
  \omega = \sqrt {\frac{5}{7}g/R} \\
  \nu = R\omega = \sqrt {5g\frac{R}{7}} \\
\end{gathered} $

Question: A double convex lens made of glass of refractive index 1.5 and radii of curvature of the curved surfaces 20 cm each is immersed in a liquid of refractive index ${n_{\text{L}}}$. The correct plot showing the variation of the power, in the units of diopter (D), as a function of ${n_L}$ is :
Solution:
$\frac{1}{{\text{f}}} = \left( {\frac{{1.5}}{{\text{n}}} - 1} \right)\left( {\frac{1}{{0.2}} - \frac{1}{{ - 0.2}}} \right) = \left( {\frac{{1.5}}{{\text{n}}} - 1} \right) \cdot 10$
Case – 1: If ${\text{P}} = \frac{1}{{\text{f}}}$
$P = \frac{{15}}{n} - 10$
Then, correct (Ans. A)
OR
Case – 2: If $P = \frac{n}{f}$
Then ${\text{P}} = \left( {1.5 - {\text{n}}} \right) \cdot 10$
Then, correct (Ans. B)

Question:
In the List-I, four optical effects are mentioned. The physical phenomena of light which are essential to describe these optical effects are given in List-II. Choose the option which describes the correct match between the entries in List-I to those in List-II.
List – I List – II
(P)Colorful sky in north polar region (Aurora Borealis)(1)Dispersion and reflection
(Q)Partially polarized sun light(2)Total internal reflection
(R)Rainbow(3)Diffraction
(S)Dark and bright fringes(4)Scattering of light by molecules in the atmosphere
(5)Emission of radiation from oxygen and nitrogen atoms excited by charged particles
Solution:
(P) Aurora Borealis is a phenomenon in which a natural light is seen in Earth's upper atmosphere caused by the charged particles from Sun colliding with atoms in the atmospheric. These collisions excite oxygen and nitrogen, which then emits light of different colours such as green, red and purple.
$\left( {\text{P}} \right) \to \left( 5 \right)$
(Q) Partially Polarised Sun light occurs due to the scattering of light by dust molecules in the atmosphere.
$\left( {\text{Q}} \right) \to \left( 4 \right)$
(R) Rainbow formation occurs due to the dispersion and reflection of light inside the water molecules in air.
$\left( {\text{R}} \right) \to \left( 1 \right)$
(S) Dark and bright fringes occur due to interference of light such as YDSE or diffraction.
$\left( {\text{S}} \right) \to \left( 3 \right)$
Ans. (A)

(P)(1)
(Q)(2)
(R)(3)
(S)(4)
(5)
Solution:
For a general loop of $\theta $ angle initially making ${\theta _0}$ angle with field.

and remains constant for next ${{\text{t}}_{{\text{constant}}}} = \frac{\theta }{\omega } = \frac{\theta }{{2\pi }}{\text{T}}$

Question:
List-I shows four configurations made of straight and semi-circular narrow tubes containing air.
A sound wave of wavelength $\lambda = 0.29{\text{m}}$ enters these structures at the point $S$ and a sound detector is placed at $D$. Between the points $S$ and $D$, the sound travels only through the tubes. List-II contains the possible smallest values of $l$ (refer to the figures) for which the detector $D$ records maximum amplitude. Ignore effects of sharp corners. [Given ${\text{cos}}\left( {15^\circ } \right) = 0.97$ ]
Choose the option that best describes the match between the entries in List-I to those in List-II.
List – I List – II
(P)(1)1.32 m
(Q)(2)1.19 m
(R)(3)0.51 m
(S)(4)0.29 m
(5)0.13 m
Solution:
(P) Path difference :$\Delta x = \pi R - 2R = \left( {\pi - 2} \right)\frac{\ell }{2}$
For maxima $\left( {\pi - 2} \right)\frac{\ell }{2} = {\text{n}}\lambda $
$${\ell _{\min \;}} = \frac{{2\lambda }}{{\pi  - 2}} = 0.51\;m$$

(Q) Path difference
$$\Delta X = 2\ell  - \ell  = \ell $$
For maxima
$\ell = {\text{n}}\lambda $
${\ell _{{\text{min}}}} = \lambda $
$ = 0.29{\text{m}}$

(R) Path difference :
$$\Delta X = \ell  + \pi R - \ell $$
$\pi {\text{R}} = \frac{{\pi \ell }}{{\sqrt 2 }}$
For maxima $\frac{{\pi \ell }}{{\sqrt 2 }} = {\text{n}}\lambda $
$ \Rightarrow {\ell _{{\text{min}}}} = \frac{{\sqrt 2 \lambda }}{\pi } = 0.13{\text{m}}$

(S) $$\Delta X = \left[ {\left( {\sqrt 2  + 2} \right) = \left( {\sqrt 3  + 1} \right)} \right]x = 0.682x$$
And $$x + \sqrt 3 x = \ell  \Rightarrow x = 0.366\ell $$
$$\Delta x = 0.249\ell $$
For maxima $0.249\ell = {\text{n}}\lambda $
$ \Rightarrow {\ell _{{\text{min}}}} = \lambda /0.249 \approx 1.19$

Question:
In a solvent S, a compound B is partially dissociated into C and D as given below :
B ⇌ 2C+ 2D
B, C and D are non-volatile in nature. The molar mass of B is 10 times the molar mass of S. The standard boiling point and the standard enthalpy of vaporization of S are 400 K and 10 R J mol–1, respectively (R is the gas constant in J K–1 mol–1). A solution of B in S with an initial concentration of B as 0.25% (mass/mass) has a boiling point of 408 K at 1 bar pressure. In this solution, the mole percent of B that has been dissociated is _____.
Solution:
\[{\text{B}} \rightleftharpoons \underbrace {2{\text{C}} + 2{\text{D}}}_{n = 4}\]
\[i = 1 + (4 - 1)\alpha \]
\[i = 1 + 3\alpha \]
\[{\text{B is }}0.25\% \frac{{{\text{ Mass }}}}{{{\text{ Mass }}}}\] ⇒ 100 g solution contain 0.25 g of B
\[{\text{Molality }} = \frac{{\frac{{0.25}}{{{{\text{M}}_{\text{B}}}}}}}{{99.75}} \times 1000 = \frac{{1000}}{{399{{\text{M}}_{\text{B}}}}}\]
Elevation of B.P. = 408 – 400 = 8 K
\[\left( {{{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}}} \right) = \frac{{{\text{R}}{{\left( {{\text{T}}_{\text{b}}^{\text{o}}} \right)}^2}{\text{M}}}}{{1000\Delta {{\text{H}}_{{\text{vap}}}}}} = \frac{{R \times {{(400)}^2} \times {{\text{M}}_{\text{s}}}}}{{1000 \times 10{\text{R}}}}\]
Kb = 16 MS
∆Tb = i × Kb × m
\[8 = (1 + 3\alpha ) \times 16\,{{\text{M}}_{\text{S}}} \times \frac{{1000}}{{399\,{{\text{M}}_{\text{B}}}}}\]
\[\because {{\text{M}}_{\text{B}}} = 10\,{{\text{M}}_{\text{S}}}\]
\[8 = (1 + 3\alpha ) \times 16\,{{\text{M}}_{\text{S}}} \times \frac{{1000}}{{399 \times 10\,{{\text{M}}_{\text{S}}}}}\]
\[1.995 = 1 + 3\alpha \]
\[\alpha = 0.3316666 \ldots \]
\[\% \alpha = 33.1666 \ldots \]

Question: Xa+ and Yb+ are hydrogen-like species. The wavelength of light absorbed during the transition between the states with principal quantum numbers n = 1 and n = 2 of Xa+ is λ. The wavelength of light absorbed during the transition between the states with principal quantum numbers n = 2 and n = 4 of Yb+ is 9λ. The lowest possible value of (a + b) is ____
Solution:
\[\frac{1}{\lambda } = {{\text{R}}_{\text{H}}}{{\text{Z}}^2}\left[ {\frac{1}{{{\text{n}}_1^2}} - \frac{1}{{{\text{n}}_2^2}}} \right]\]
\[{\text{For }}{{\text{X}}^{{\text{a}} + }} = \frac{1}{\lambda } = {{\text{R}}_{\text{H}}}{\left( {{{\text{z}}_{\text{x}}}} \right)^2}\left\{ {\frac{1}{{{1^2}}} - \frac{1}{{{2^2}}}} \right\}\]
\[{\text{For }}{{\text{Y}}^{{\text{b}} + }} = \frac{1}{\lambda } = {{\text{R}}_{\text{H}}}{\left( {{{\text{z}}_{\text{y}}}} \right)^2}\left\{ {\frac{1}{{{2^2}}} - \frac{1}{{{4^2}}}} \right\}\]
Equation (1) ÷ Equation (2)
\[9 = \frac{{z_x^2}}{{z_y^2}} \times \frac{{3/4}}{{3/16}}\]
\[\frac{{z_x^2}}{{z_y^2}} = \frac{9}{4}\]
\[\frac{{{z_x}}}{{{z_y}}} = \frac{3}{2}\]
For minimum values = zx = 3, zy = 2
Xa+ and Yb+ are hydrogen like species.
So, a should be 2 & b should be 1.
Lowest possible value of a + b = 2 + 1 = 3

Question:
At a given temperature, 0.45 g of acetic acid in 50 mL of water is shaken with 1.0 g of charcoal and the pH of the resulting solution is 3.0. Assume, the adsorption of acetic acid from the aqueous solution by charcoal follows Freundlich isotherm,
\[\frac{x}{m} = k{C^{l/n}}\]
If the plot of log10(x/m) against log10C gives a straight line with slope 1, the value of k in L mol–1 is ____.
Given: The molar mass of acetic acid is 60 g mol–1.
The acid dissociation constant of acetic acid is 1.0 × 10–5 at the given temperature.
x is the mass (in grams) of acetic acid adsorbed.
 m is the mass (in grams) of charcoal
C is the equilibrium concentration of acetic acid in the solution after the adsorption is complete.
k and n are constants for acetic acid-charcoal system at the given temperature.
Solution:
Initial moles of CH3COOH \[ = \frac{{0.45}}{{60}} = 0.0075\]
Initial molarity of CH3COOH \[ = \frac{{0.0075}}{{50}} \times 1000 = 0.15\,{\text{M}}\]
After adsorption given \[{\text{pH}} = 3 \Rightarrow \left[ {{{\text{H}}^ + }} \right] = {10^{ - 3}}{\text{ M}}\]
\[{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3}{\text{COOH}}({\text{aq}}) \rightleftharpoons {\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3}{\text{CO}}{{\text{O}}^ - }({\text{aq}}) + {{\text{H}}^ + }({\text{aq}})\]
\[{{\text{K}}_{\text{a}}} = 1.0 \times {10^5} = \frac{{{{10}^{ - 3}} \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{{\text{C}} - {{10}^{ - 3}}}}\]
C=0.1 M
Adsorbed mass of CH3COOH on 1 g charcoal\[ = \frac{{(0.15 - 0.1) \times 50 \times 60}}{{1000}} = 0.15\;{\text{g}}\]
\[\log \frac{{\text{x}}}{{\text{m}}} = \log {\text{K}} + \frac{1}{{\text{n}}}\log {\text{C}}\]
log 0.15 = log K + 1 × log0.1
log 1.5 = log K
K = 1.5

Question: Natural rubber on complete ozonolysis (O3/Zn-H2O) gives compound X as the major product. X gives positive iodoform and Tollen’s tests. X on heating with aqueous NaOH gives Y as the major product. Y is

Consider the following reaction sequence in which J, K, L and M are the major products.

Given: Atomic mass (in amu): H : 1, C : 12, N : 14, O : 16, S : 32, Br : 80, Ba : 137

In sulphur estimation by Carius method, the amount of BaSO4 formed from 3.79 g of M is ____ g.
Solution:
Molecular formula of M = C22 H21 NO3S
Molar mass of M = 379 gm/mol
Given mass of M = 3.79 gm
Hence Moles of M = \[\frac{{3.79}}{{379}}\] = 0.01
Molecule of M contains 1 sulphur atom so 0.01 mole of BaSO4 is formed
Mass of BaSO4 = 0.01 × 233 = 2.33 g

Question:
At 300 K, the molar conductivities of the aqueous solutions of three salts at two different concentrations are given below :
SaltConcentration (M)Molar conductivity (S cm2 mol–1)
NaNO30.01 111
0.04 101
NaCl0.01 117
0.04 107
AgNO30.01 125
0.04 116

The conductivity of a saturated aqueous solution of AgCl is 1.40 × 10–6 S cm–1 at 300 K. If the solubility of AgCl in water at 300 K is X mol L–1, then log10(X–1) is
(Assume that AgCl dissolved in water ionizes completely and that the molar conductivity of saturated AgCl solution is equal to its limiting molar conductivity.)
Solution:
For NaNO3
\[111 = - - b\sqrt {0.01} + \Lambda _m^o\]
\[111 = - 0.1b + \Lambda _m^o\]…..(i)
\[101 = - b\sqrt {0.04} + \Lambda _m^o\]
\[101 = - 0.2b + \Lambda _m^o\]…..(ii)
\[\Lambda _{m\left( {{\text{NaN}}{{\text{O}}_3}} \right)}^o = 121\,{\text{Sc}}{{\text{m}}^2}\;{\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - 1}}\]
For NaCl
\[117 = - 0.1\;{\text{b}} + \Lambda _{\text{m}}^{\text{o}}\]
\[107 = - 0.2\;{\text{b}} + \Lambda _{\text{m}}^{\text{o}}\]
\[\Lambda _{{\text{m}}({\text{NaCl}})}^{\text{o}} = 127\,{\text{Sc}}{{\text{m}}^2}\;{\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - 1}}\]
For AgNO3
\[125 = - 0.1\;{\text{b}} + \Lambda _{\text{m}}^{\text{o}}\]
\[116 = - 0.2\;{\text{b}} + \Lambda _{\text{m}}^{\text{o}}\]
\[\Lambda _{{\text{m}}\left( {{\text{AgN}}{{\text{O}}_3}} \right)}^{\text{o}} = 134\,{\text{Sc}}{{\text{m}}^2}\;{\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - 1}}\]
\[\Lambda _{{\text{m}}({\text{AgCl}})}^{\text{o}} = 127 + 134 - 121 = 140\]
\[140 = \frac{{1.40 \times {{10}^{ - 6}} \times 1000}}{{\;{\text{S}}}}\]
\[{\text{X}} = {\text{S}} = {10^{ - 5}}\,{\text{M}}\]
\[{\log _{10}}{\text{x}} = 5\]

Question:
Two volatile liquids A and B form an ideal solution. Consider a 5 molal solution of B in A inside a closed container having a total vapour pressure of 100 mm Hg at 300 K. The vapour pressure of pure A at 300K is 105 mm Hg. Assume that A and B behave as ideal gases in the vapour phase.
Given:
The gas constant R = 0.08 L atm K–1 mol–1
Molar mass of A is 50 g mol–1
Molar mass of B is 57 g mol–1
Density of liquid B at 300 K is 0.5 g/mL
1 atm = 760 mm Hg

At 300 K, the ratio of the molar volume of pure B in vapour phase to its molar volume in liquid phase is __________
Solution:
\[{{\text{P}}_{\text{T}}} = {{\text{X}}_{\text{A}}}{\text{P}}_{\text{A}}^{\text{o}} + {{\text{X}}_{\text{B}}}{\text{P}}_{\text{B}}^{\text{o}}\]
\[100 = \frac{{1000/50}}{{\frac{{1000}}{{50}} + 5}}(105) + \frac{5}{{\frac{{1000}}{{50}} + 5}}\left( {{\text{P}}_{\text{B}}^{\text{o}}} \right)\]
\[{\text{P}}_{\text{B}}^{\text{o}}\]= 80 mm of Hg
Molar volume of vapour of B
\[{{\text{V}}_{\text{m}}} = \frac{{{\text{RT}}}}{{\text{P}}} = \frac{{0.08 \times 300}}{{80/760}} = 228\,{\text{lit}}/{\text{mol}}\]
Molar volume of B in liquid phase
\[{\text{V}}{'_{\text{m}}} = \frac{{57}}{{0.5}} = 114{\text{ml}}/{\text{mol}}\]
\[{\text{Ratio}} = \frac{{{\text{molar volume in vapour}}}}{{{\text{molar volume in liquid}}}} = \frac{{228 \times 1000}}{{114}} = 2000\]

For a first-order reaction R ⟶ P at a given temperature, k is the rate constant. For this reaction, at the given temperature, the concentrations of R and P at a time t are [R] and [P], respectively. The correct graphical representation(s) for this reaction is(are)
Solution:
A) \[{[{\text{P}}]_{\text{t}}} = {[{\text{R}}]_0}\left( {1 - {{\text{e}}^{ - {\text{kt}}}}} \right)\]

B) \[{\text{Rate}} = \frac{{ - {\text{d}}[{\text{R}}]}}{{{\text{dt}}}} = {\text{k}}[{\text{R}}]\]
\[\frac{{{\text{d}}[{\text{R}}]}}{{{\text{dt}}}} = - {\text{k}}[{\text{R}}]\]
\[y = - {\text{mx}}\]

C) \[{\text{Rate}} = \frac{{ - {\text{d}}[{\text{R}}]}}{{{\text{dt}}}} = \frac{{{\text{d}}[{\text{P}}]}}{{{\text{dt}}}} = {\text{k}}[{\text{R}}]\]
\[{\text{So, }}\frac{{{\text{d}}[{\text{P}}]}}{{{\text{dt}}}} = {\text{k}}{[{\text{R}}]_0}{{\text{e}}^{ - {\text{kt}}}}\]

D) k = constant
