${\text{a}}{{\text{x}}^2} + {\text{bx}} + {\text{c}} = 0$ has only integer solution $\alpha ,\beta $
${\text{a}}{{\text{x}}^2} + {\text{bx}} + {\text{c}} = {\text{a}}\left( {{\text{x}} - \alpha } \right)\left( {{\text{x}} - \beta } \right)$
put ${\text{x}} = - 2$
$\left( {\alpha + 2} \right)\left( {\beta + 2} \right) = 3$
If ${\text{a}},{\text{b}},{\text{c}} > 0 \Rightarrow \alpha ,\beta < 0$
$\left( {\alpha + 2} \right)\left( {\beta + 2} \right) = - 1 \times - 3$
$ = - 3 \times - 1$
$\left( {\alpha ,\beta } \right) = \left( { - 3, - 5} \right);\left( { - 5, - 3} \right)$
${\text{b}} = 8{\text{a}};{\text{c}} = 15{\text{a}}$
Now verify the options

Question:
Let $\mathbb{R}$ denote the set of all real numbers. Consider the polynomial function $f:\mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ defined by \[f\left( x \right) = \frac{{{d^{10}}}}{{d{x^{10}}}}\left( {{{\left( {{x^2} - 1} \right)}^{10}}} \right),\,\,{\text{for}}\,\,{\text{all}}\,\,\,x \in \mathbb{R}\].
Here $\frac{{{d^{10}}}}{{d{x^{10}}}}\left( {{{\left( {{x^2} - 1} \right)}^{10}}} \right)$ is the 10th order derivative of the function ${\left( {{x^2} - 1} \right)^{10}}$.
Then which of the following statements is (are) TRUE ?
Options: The coefficient of ${x^8}$ in the polynomial $f\left( x \right)$ is $\left( { - 10} \right)\left( {\frac{{18!}}{{8!}}} \right)$
The value of $f\left( 1 \right) + f\left( { - 1} \right)$ is equal to $10!{2^{11}}$
The degree of the polynomial $f\left( x \right)$ is 10
The constant term of the polynomial $f\left( x \right)$ is $ - \left( {\frac{{10!}}{{5!}}} \right)$
Solution:
${\text{P}}\left( {\text{x}} \right) = {\left( {{{\text{x}}^2} - 1} \right)^{10}}$
⇒ Degree $ = 20$
${\text{f}}\left( {\text{x}} \right) = {{\text{P}}^{10}}\left( {\text{x}} \right)$
Option C
when we differentiate 10-times ; Degree of ${\text{f}}\left( {\text{x}} \right) = 20 - 10 = 10$
$P\left( x \right) = \sum\limits_{r = 0}^{10} {{\,^{10}}{C_r}} \,{x^{20 - 2r}}{\left( { - 1} \right)^r}$
Option A
$20 - 2{\text{r}} - 10 = 8 \Rightarrow {\text{r}} = 1$
$f\left( x \right) = ...\underbrace { - {\,^{10}}{C_1} \cdot \left( {18.17.16...9} \right) \cdot {x^8}}_{ - {\;^{10}}{{\text{C}}_1} \cdot \frac{{18!}}{{8!}}}$
Option D
$20 - 2{\text{r}} - 10 = 0 \Rightarrow {\text{r}} = 5$
${\text{f}}\left( {\text{x}} \right) = \ldots - {\;^{10}}{{\text{C}}_5}\left( {10.9.8 \ldots 3.2.1} \right)$
$ = - {\;^{10}}{{\text{C}}_5}.10!$
Option B
${\text{P}}\left( {\text{x}} \right) = {({\text{x}} - 1)^{10}}{({\text{x}} + 1)^{10}}$
${{\text{P}}^{10}}\left( 1 \right) = {2^{10}}.10! = {\text{f}}\left( 1 \right)$
${{\text{P}}^{10}}\left( { - 1} \right) = 10!{.2^{10}} = {\text{f}}\left( { - 1} \right)$
${\text{f}}\left( 1 \right) + {\text{f}}\left( { - 1} \right) = {2^{11}}.10!$

Question:
A thermal power plant produces electric power of 600 kW at 4000 V, which is to be transported to a place 20 km away from the power plant for consumers’ usage. It can be transported either directly with a cable of large current carrying capacity or by using a combination of step-up and step-down transformers at the two ends. The drawback of the direct transmission is the large energy dissipation. In the method using transformers, the dissipation is much smaller. In this method, a step-up transformer is used at the plant side so that the current is reduced to a smaller value. At the consumers’ end, a step-down transformer is used to supply power to the consumers at the specified lower voltage. It is reasonable to assume that the power cable is purely resistive and the transformers are ideal with the power factor unity. All the currents and voltage mentioned are rms values.


If the direct transmission method with a cable of resistance \[0.4\Omega k{m^{ - 1}}\] is used, the power dissipation (in % ) during transmission is
Solution:
For direct transmission \[P = {i^2}R = {(150)^2}(0.4 \times 20) = 1.8 \times {10^5}w\]
fraction(in % ) \[ = \frac{{1.8 \times {{10}^5}}}{{6 \times {{10}^5}}} \times 100 = 30\% \]

Question:
A small block of mass 1 kg is released from rest at the top of a rough track. The track is circular arc of radius 40 m. The block slides along the track without toppling and a frictional force acts on it in the direction opposite to the instantaneous velocity. The work done in overcoming the friction up to the point Q, as shown in the figure below, is 150 J. (Take the acceleration due to gravity, g = 10 m/s2).

The speed of the block when it reaches the point Q is
Solution:
Using work energy theorem 
\[mgR\sin 30^\circ  + {W_f} = \frac{1}{2}m{v^2}\]
\[200 - 150 = \frac{{{v^2}}}{2}\]
\[ \Rightarrow v = 10m/s\]

Q. If $\cos x + \cos y + \cos z = 0 = \sin x + \sin y + \sin z$ then possible value of $\cos \frac{{x - y}}{2}$ is2. $\sqrt 2 $

Solution:
\[\begin{gathered}
  P.{\left\{ {\frac{1}{{{y^2}}}{{\left( {\frac{{\cos \left( {{{\tan }^{ - 1}}y} \right) + y\sin \left( {{{\tan }^{ - 1}}y} \right)}}{{\cot \left( {{{\sin }^{ - 1}}y} \right) + \tan \left( {{{\sin }^{ - 1}}y} \right)}}} \right)}^2} + {y^4}} \right\}^{\frac{1}{2}}} \\
   = {\left\{ {\frac{1}{{y^2}}{{\left( {\frac{{\frac{1}{{\sqrt {1 + {y^2}} }} + \frac{{{y^2}}}{{\sqrt {1 + {y^2}} }}}}{{\frac{{\sqrt {1 - {y^2}} }}{y} + \frac{y}{{\sqrt {1 - {y^2}} }}}}} \right)}^2} + {y^4}} \right\}^{\frac{1}{2}}} \\

Q. If \[\cos x + \cos y + \cos z = \sin x + \sin y + \sin z = 0\]
$\cos x + \cos y = - \cos z$
$\sin x + \sin y = - \sin z$
On squaring and adding
$2 + 2\cos \left( {x - y} \right) = 1$
$\cos \left( {x - y} \right) = - \frac{1}{2}$
$2{\cos ^2}\left( {\frac{{x - y}}{2}} \right) - 1 = - \frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow \cos \left( {\frac{{x - y}}{2}} \right) = \frac{1}{2}$

or \[1 + 6{x^2} = 6 - 6{x^2}\]

Question:
A small block of mass 1 kg is released from rest at the top of a rough track. The track is circular arc of radius 40 m. The block slides along the track without toppling and a frictional force acts on it in the direction opposite to the instantaneous velocity. The magnitude of the normal reaction that acts on the block at the point Q is
Solution:
\[N - mg\cos 60^\circ  = \frac{{m{v^2}}}{R}\]

Question:
For a > b > c > 0, the distance between (1, 1) and the point of intersection of the
lines ax + by + c = 0 and bx + ay + c = 0 is less than $2\sqrt 2 .$ Then,
Solution:
As a > b > c > 0
a > b > 0 and b > 0
⇒ a + b − c > 0,
So (d) is wrong
Using the same relation, (c) is wrong

a − b > 0 and c > 0 .... (ii)
a + c – b > 0
So (b) is wrong

So (a) must be correct

Alternate method

The point of intersection for ax + by + c = 0 and bx + ay + c = 0
i.e. $\left( {\frac{{ - c}}{{a + b}},\frac{{ - c}}{{a + b}}} \right)$
The distance between (1, 1) and $\left( {\frac{{ - c}}{{a + b}},\frac{{ - c}}{{a + b}}} \right)$is less than $2\sqrt 2 $
$ \Rightarrow \sqrt {{{\left( {1 + \frac{c}{{a + b}}} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {1 + \frac{c}{{a + b}}} \right)}^2}} < 2\sqrt 2 $
$ \Rightarrow \left( {\frac{{a + b + c}}{{a + b}}} \right)\sqrt 2 < 2\sqrt 2 $
$ \Rightarrow a + b + c < 2a + 2b$
$ \Rightarrow a + b - c > 0$

Question: A pack contains n card numbered from 1 to n. Two consecutive numbered cards are removed from the pack and the sum of the numbers on the remaining cards is 1224. If the smaller of the numbers on the removed cards is k, then k – 20 is equal to
Solution:
Let k, k + 1 be removed from pack
$\therefore \left( {1 + 2 + 3 + ..... + n} \right) - \left( {k + k + 1} \right) = 1224$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{n\left( {n + 1} \right)}}{2} - 2k = 1225$
$ \Rightarrow k = \frac{{n\left( {n + 1} \right) - 2450}}{4}$
${\text{for }}n = 50,k = 25$
$ \Rightarrow k - 20 = 5$

A thermal power plant produces electric power of 600 kW at 4000 V, which is to be transported to a place 20 km away from the power plant for consumers’ usage. It can be transported either directly with a cable of large current carrying capacity or by using a combination of step-up and step-down transformers at the two ends. The drawback of the direct transmission is the large energy dissipation. In the method using transformers, the dissipation is much smaller. In this method, a step-up transformer is used at the plant side so that the current is reduced to a smaller value. At the consumers’ end, a step-down transformer is used to supply power to the consumers at the specified lower voltage. It is reasonable to assume that the power cable is purely resistive and the transformers are ideal with the power factor unity. All the currents and voltage mentioned are rms values.


In the method using the transformers, assume that the ratio of the number of turns in the primary to that in the secondary in the step-up transformer is 1: 10. Question: For 3 × 3 matrices M and N, which of the following statement (s) is (are) not correct?
Options: N<sup>T</sup>M N is symmetric or skew-symmetric, according as M is symmetric or skew-symmetric
MN-NM is skew symmetric for all symmetric matrices M and N
M N is symmetric for all symmetric matrices M and N
(adj M) (adj N) = adj (MN) for all invertible matrices M and N
Solution:
a) (NTMN)T = NTMT(NT)T = NTMTN, is symmetric if M is symmetric and skew-symmetric, if M is skew - symmetric.
b) (MN - NM)T = (MN)T – (NM)T
= NM – MN = – (MN – NM)
∴ skew-symmetric, when M and N are symmetric.
c) (MN)T = NTMT = NM ≠ MN
∴ Not correct
d) (adj MN) = (adj N). (adj M)
∴ Not correct.

Question: A vertical line passing through the point (h, 0) intersects the ellipse $\frac{{{x^2}}}{4} + \frac{{{y^2}}}{3} = 1$ at the points P and Q. Let the tangents to the ellipse at P and Q meet at the point R. If \[\Delta \left( h \right) = {\text{area of the }}\Delta {\text{PQR,}}\,{\Delta _{\text{1}}} = \mathop {\max }\limits_{1/2 \leqslant h \leqslant 1} \Delta \left( h \right){\text{ and }}{\Delta _{\text{2}}} = \mathop {\min }\limits_{1/2 \leqslant h \leqslant 1} \Delta \left( h \right),{\text{ then }}\frac{8}{{\sqrt 5 }}{\Delta _1} - 8{\Delta _2}\]
Solution:
As for lower value of h, base PQ and height both are greater for triangle PQR, so maximum area is for h=1/2 and minimum area is for h=1
By observation, area will be maximum for
To find area in terms of parametric coordinates
Here, tangent at $P\left( {2\cos \theta ,\sqrt 3 \sin \theta } \right)$ is

\[\frac{x}{2}\cos \theta + \frac{y}{{\sqrt 3 }}\sin \theta = 1\]
\[\therefore R\left( {2\sec \theta ,0} \right)\]
\[ \Rightarrow \Delta = {\text{Area of }}\Delta {\text{PQR}}\]
\[{\text{ = }}\frac{1}{2}\left( {2\sqrt 3 \sin \theta } \right)\left( {2\sec \theta - 2\cos \theta } \right)\]
\[ = 2\sqrt 3 \frac{{{{\sin }^3}\theta }}{{\cos \theta }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,....({\text{i}})\]
\[\therefore {\Delta _1} = {\Delta _{\max }}\,\,{\text{occurs at cos}}\theta {\text{ = }}\frac{1}{4} = \left( {\frac{{2\sqrt 3 .{{\sin }^3}\theta }}{{\cos \theta }}} \right)\]
\[{\text{When }}\cos \theta = \frac{1}{4} = \frac{{45\sqrt 5 }}{8}\]
\[{\Delta _2} = {\Delta _{\min }}{\text{ occurs at cos}}\theta {\text{ = }}\frac{1}{2} = \left( {\frac{{2\sqrt 3 {{\sin }^3}\theta }}{{\cos \theta }}} \right)\]
\[{\text{When}}\,\cos \theta = \frac{1}{2} = \frac{9}{2}\]
\[\therefore \frac{8}{{\sqrt 5 }}{\Delta _1} - 8{\Delta _1} = 45 - 36 = 9\]

Question:
Match List-I with List-II and select the correct answer using the code given below the lists
List-IList-II
P. Volume of parallelepiped determined by vectors $\vec a,\vec b$ and $\vec c$ is 2. Then the volume of the parallelepiped determined by vectors $2\left( {\vec a \times \vec b} \right),3\left( {\vec b \times \vec c} \right)$ and $\left( {\vec c \times \vec a} \right)$ is1. 100
Q. Volume of parallelepiped determined by vectors $\vec a,\vec b$ and $\vec c$ is 5. Then the volume of the parallelepiped determined by vectors $3\left( {\vec a + \vec b} \right),\left( {\vec b + \vec c} \right)$ and $2\left( {\vec c + \vec a} \right)$ is2. 30
R. Area of a triangle with adjacent sides determined by vectors $\vec a$ and $\vec b$ is 20. Then the area of the triangle with adjacent sides determined by vectors $\left( {2\vec a + 3\vec b} \right)$ and $\left( {\vec a - \vec b} \right)$ is3. 24
S. Area of a parallelogram with adjacent sides determined by vectors $\vec a$ and $\vec b$ is 30. Then the area of the parallelogram with adjacent sides determined by vectors $\left( {\vec a + \vec b} \right)$ and $\vec a$ is 4. 60
Solution:
(P) Given, \[\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\bar a}&{\bar b}&{\bar c}
\end{array}} \right] = 2\]
Now, \[V = \left[ {2\left( {\bar a \times \bar b} \right)\,\,\,3\left( {\bar b \times \bar c} \right)\,\,\,\left( {\bar c \times \bar a} \right)} \right]\]
\[\begin{gathered}
   = 6{\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\bar a}&{\bar b}&{\bar c}
\end{array}} \right]^2} \\
   = 24 \\
\end{gathered} \]
(Q) Given, \[\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\bar a}&{\bar b}&{\bar c}
\end{array}} \right] = 5\]
Now, Volume
\[\begin{gathered}
   = 6\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\bar a + \bar b}&{\bar b + \bar c}&{\bar c + \bar a}
\end{array}} \right] \\
   = 12\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\bar a}&{\bar b}&{\bar c}
\end{array}} \right] \\
   = 60 \\
\end{gathered} \]
(R) Given, \[\left| {\bar a \times \bar b} \right| = 40\]
Now, \[A = \frac{1}{2}\left| {\left( {2\bar a + 3\bar b} \right) \times \left( {\bar a - \bar b} \right)} \right|\]
\[\begin{gathered}
   = \frac{1}{2}.5\left| {\bar a \times \bar b} \right| \\
   = \frac{5}{2} \times 40 \\
   = 100 \\
\end{gathered} \]
(S) Given, \[\left| {\bar a \times \bar b} \right| = 30\]
Now, \[A = \left| {\left( {\bar a + \bar b} \right) \times \bar a} \right|\]
\[\begin{gathered}
   = \left| {\bar a \times \bar b} \right| \\
   = 30 \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question:
Of the three independent events E1, E2, and E3, the probability that only E1 occurs is α, only E2 occurs is β and only E3 occurs is γ. Let the probability p that none of events E1, E2 or E3 occurs satisfy the equations
$\left( {\alpha - 2\beta } \right)p = \alpha \beta {\text{ and }}\left( {\beta - 3\gamma } \right)p = 2\beta \gamma .$ All the given probabilities are assumed to lie in the interval (0, 1). Then $\frac{{{\text{Probability of occurrence of }}{{\text{E}}_{\text{1}}}}}{{{\text{Probability of occurrence of }}{{\text{E}}_{\text{3}}}}} = \_\_\_\_$
Solution:
Let P(E1) = x, P (E2) = y and P (E3) = z
then (1 − x) (1 − y) (1 − z) = p
x (1 − y) (1 − z) = α
(1 − x) y (1 − z) = β
(1 − x) (1 − y) z = γ
So $\frac{{1 - x}}{x} = \frac{p}{\alpha }\,;\,\,x = \frac{\alpha }{{\alpha + p}}$
Similarly $z = \frac{\gamma }{{\gamma + p}}$
So $ = \frac{{P\left( {{E_1}} \right)}}{{P\left( {{E_3}} \right)}} = \frac{{\frac{\alpha }{{\alpha + p}}}}{{\frac{\gamma }{{\gamma + p}}}} = \frac{{\frac{{\gamma + p}}{\gamma }}}{{\frac{{\alpha + p}}{\alpha }}} = \frac{{1 + \frac{p}{\gamma }}}{{1 + \frac{p}{\alpha }}}$
also given = $\frac{{\alpha \beta }}{{\alpha - 2\beta }} = p = \frac{{2\beta \gamma }}{{\beta - 3\gamma }} \Rightarrow \beta = \frac{{5\alpha \gamma }}{{\alpha + 4\gamma }}$
Substituting back
 $\left( {\alpha - 2\left( {\frac{{5\alpha \gamma }}{{\alpha + 4\gamma }}} \right)} \right)p = \frac{{\alpha .5\alpha \gamma }}{{\alpha + 4\gamma }}$
$ \Rightarrow \alpha p - 6p\gamma = 5\alpha \gamma $
$ \Rightarrow \left( {\frac{p}{\gamma } + 1} \right) = 6\left( {\frac{p}{\alpha } + 1} \right) \Rightarrow \frac{{\frac{p}{\gamma } + 1}}{{\frac{p}{\alpha } + 1}} = 6$
